items.title: Syllabus for GEN101: Developing Skills & Strategies for Success (GEA2602D)
items.plainText:
Course Syllabus

Course Syllabus

Course Description

In this foundational course, students explore the principles necessary for achieving personal and career success. GEN101 serves as a road map, guiding students as they begin their academic journey. Through self-discovery, surveying available resources, connecting with UAGC groups, and engaging with Career Services, students learn the essential skills of planning and goal setting. Students apply their personal strengths, skills, and lifelong learning strategies to develop essential career competencies. By making these meaningful connections students gain a deeper understanding of how their education relates to their desired career path. Congratulations on embarking on this college journey filled with growth, exploration, and endless possibilities!

Course Design

This course is the launching point for new UAGC students. Students can look forward to discovering many useful resources and supports to aid them in their college journey. Concepts such as growth mindset, grit, goal setting, and learning strategies are scaffolded across five weeks with opportunities to make learning transferable and concrete. Each week, students will apply their learning to design a personal development plan. The Personal Development Plan final project is a practical and useful document to be revisited and adjusted as students continue their learning journey.

This engaging course provokes critical thinking and contemplation of one’s learning with collaborative Reflective Thinking forums and weekly discussions with peers and their instructor. Student supports, such as assignment templates, are built into all the weeks. Additionally, students will create a free electronic portfolio account to store their best or favorite work samples in this course and other UAGC courses. This ePortfolio will serve as a social, academic, and professional platform for students to share their learning, experiences, and skills with others.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to

• Apply skills to promote personal, academic, and professional development.

• Utilize resources and tools to promote success.

• Evaluate how lifelong learning strategies can promote academic and professional success.

• Create a plan for personal growth, including career and academic development.

Course Map

The course map illustrates the careful design of the course through which each learning outcome is supported by one or more specific learning activities in order to create integrity and pedagogical depth in the learning experience. In the corresponding right-hand column, list all graded and non-graded activities (all items that appear in the weekly overview sections, but not the readings or other resources) that support the course learning outcomes. In the middle column, beside each activity, list the week in which the activity appears in the course.

Institutional Learning Outcomes

The University’s Institutional Learning Outcomes (ILOs) represent the knowledge, skills, and abilities that students are expected to develop through their experiences at the university regardless of their course of study or level of degree. The ILOs are designed to guide the development of student learning outcomes and to provide a framework for decisions in the colleges. The ILOs were developed by a multi-disciplinary group of faculty and students with input from other academic leaders. They provide the foundation for alignment of the university’s curriculum with the core competencies of the WASC Senior College and University Commission and the National Association of Colleges and Employers (NACE).

University graduates:

• Apply logic and critical thinking to evaluate reasoning, explore diverse perspectives, and engage in metacognition.

• Communicate thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively in written and oral forms.

• Leverage digital technologies to solve problems, complete tasks, and accomplish goals.

• Demonstrate information literacy through accessing, evaluating, and using information.

• Construct ethical responses to contemporary problems.

• Cultivate knowledge of and respect for people with diverse backgrounds, values, and lifestyles.

• Apply knowledge of commonalities and differences across varying cultural, economic, and geographic populations as part of global citizenship.

• Demonstrate personal accountability using effective interpersonal communication and emotional intelligence.

Mission Statement

To provide a community of caring and guidance for academic, financial, mental health, and career support while providing a path that makes quality college education accessible to adult learners through online, flexible degree and certificate programs.

items.title: Course Grading
items.plainText:
Course Grading

COMPONENTS OF COURSE GRADING

Multiple measures of assessment are used in the course, allowing students opportunities to demonstrate their learning in more than one way and considering individual learning styles. Course components that will be assessed are noted below.

Discussion Forums

Each week students will participate in online discussions with classmates. These discussions replace the interactive dialogue that occurs in the traditional classroom setting. In Week 1, initial posts in the Post Your Introduction forum are due by 11:59 p.m. (in the time zone in which each student resides) on Day 1 (Tuesday). For all subsequent discussions, students’ initial discussion posts are due by 11:59 p.m. (in the time zone in which each student resides) on Day 3 (Thursday). Students will have until 11:59 p.m. on Day 7 (the following Monday) to make the required minimum number of response posts to classmates. Discussion forums represent 33% of the overall course grade.

Learning Activities

There are learning activities due in Weeks 1 through 4 of this course. Each activity provides learning and self-assessment throughout the interactive activity. These activities include, but are not limited to, the following:

• Interactives

• Virtual tours

• Acknowledgement Quizzes

Students will demonstrate and reinforce their understanding of the week’s Learning Activities by taking accompanying quizzes. Students have unlimited time to complete a quiz once they begin. The quiz must be completed by the due date. The quizzes are open book and open notes and consist of short answers, multiple-choice, and true or false questions. Students have unlimited time and attempts for each quiz and the highest grade will be captured in the gradebook. Learning activities represent 17% of the overall course grade.

Quizzes

In Weeks 1, 2, 3, and 5, students will demonstrate and reinforce their understanding of the week’s content by taking open-book quizzes. Students have unlimited time to complete a quiz once they begin. The quiz must be completed by the due date. The quizzes are open book and open notes and consist of short answers, multiple-choice, and true or false questions. Students have unlimited time and attempts for each quiz and the highest grade will be captured in the gradebook. Quizzes represent 13% of the overall course grade.

Journal

There is a written journal due in Week 2 of this course. This journal must reflect college-level writing and critical thinking. Journal represents 5% of the overall course grade.

Assignments

There are written assignments due in Weeks 3 and 4 of this course. Each assignment lends itself to the Week 5 Final Project: Personal Development Plan. In Week 3, students fill in an assignment template regarding their SMART (specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time bound) goals. In Week 4, students complete a Self-Assessment analysis and complete a short paper on the results of the self-assessment. These assignments must reflect college-level writing and critical thinking. Assignments represent 17% of the overall course grade.

Final Project

The final project for this course is a Personal Development Plan. The purpose of the Personal Development Plan final project is for students to demonstrate the learning achieved in the course by creating a workable plan for their academic and career-focused goals. In this Personal Development Plan, students will create professional and academic SMART goals and reflect on the impact of mindfulness, integrity, and grit on achieving their goals. The final project represents 15% of the overall course grade.

items.title: Peer Tutoring
items.plainText:
Peer Tutoring

Feeling stuck or want to connect with a fellow UAGC student? Give Peer Tutoring a Try!

This course is part of the Peer Tutoring Program at UAGC! You can meet with a peer student tutor by selecting Peer Tutoring in the left navigation menu of your Canvas course page!

What is a Peer Tutor?

Peer tutors are UAGC students who have successfully taken your course and can provide assignment- and course-specific help, tips for time management, and share some of their experiences. To learn more, review this UAGC Peer Tutoring video . [2 minutes]

How can a peer tutor help me with GEN101?

•

•

•

•

•

• Navigating the Canvas classroom.

• Explaining discussions or assignment requirements.

• Developing paragraphs.

• Assisting with a SWOT Analysis.

• Providing tips and strategies for time management.

• Connecting you with UAGC resources and supports.

• Developing strong discussion posts.

• Understanding and developing SMART goals.

• Maintaining academic integrity.

Anything else I should know?

24/7 writing and library tutoring is also available in this course. If you need additional tutoring assistance, check out the Which Academic Support Should I Use?tool to better understand your options. If you have any questions about Peer Tutoring, reach out to PeerTutoring@uagc.edu.

items.title: Faculty Expectations
items.plainText:
Faculty Expectations

Contact Information

If you have questions about the course or a question that is more specific to you (e.g. your grades, content is of personal nature, etc.), email is the best way to contact your instructor. Please refer to the email address posted in your instructor's profile. For available office hours, please review the announcements posted in the course.

Instructor's Expectations of the Students

The Learning Week:

All online courses at the University of Arizona Global Campus are divided into a series of Learning Weeks, which always begin on a Tuesday and end on a Monday. All assignment due dates refer to the end of the day (11:59 pm) in student's own time zone. When you log in to class, you will see the Course Calendar in the Course Home module, so you will always know what day an assignment is due during each week. When in doubt, feel free to ask!

Recycling Policy:

Please refer to the Student Responsibilities and Policies section under the Course Policies page in the course.

Other Course Expectations:

• Please review UAGC's Plagiarism policy. Students are expected to maintain high standards of academic integrity. Global Campus defines academic dishonesty as deceitful and/or deceptive attempts to fulfill academic requirements. While plagiarism is the most common form of academic dishonesty, cheating or furnishing fabricated or false information to university officials and faculty (such as lying to affect a grade change) are also acts of academic dishonesty and will be met with censure. The academic community of the University believes that one of the goals of an institution of higher learning is to strengthen academic integrity and responsibility among its members. To this end, the University, throughout its history, has emphasized the importance of sound judgment and a personal sense of responsibility in each student. All members of the academic community are expected to abide by the highest standards of academic integrity.

• Please review the Student Responsibilities and Policies section under the Course Policies page in the course.

• Discussion Board Requirements: please review announcements for any specific details that may be required regarding discussion participation.

• Please review UAGC's netiquette policy under the Course Policies page.

• Please refer to the Course Materials section for information on required texts and resources.

Students' Expectations of the Instructor

Response time: When can students expect to receive an answer to questions?

• The instructor will answer student emails within 48 hours.

Grades: When can students expect to see grades?

• Discussion board grades will be posted within 72 hours after the end of each week, i.e., 11:59 pm on Thursday.

• Written assignment grades and feedback are due within 6 days after the submission due date, i.e., if a student paper is due by 11:59 pm on Monday, instructor feedback and grade are due by 11:59 pm on the following Sunday.

• Final grades will be posted within 6 days after the course ends, i.e., 11:59 pm on Sunday.

Weekly Guidance: When can students expect to see Weekly Guidance posted?

• Weekly Guidance will be posted PRIOR to the beginning of each week.

Welcome to Week 1

Watch this video for an overview of GEN101.

|

Weekly Learning Outcomes

This week you will

• Create a UAGC student profile.

• Utilize strategies and tools needed for professional development.

• Identify personal strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats.

• Express ideas with peers to build community.

Introduction

On the Friday before a long holiday weekend, Eduardo (Ed) grabs his usual cup of coffee before catching the train to his job downtown at a national cell phone store. Ed is hopeful that today he’ll hear whether he’s getting the promotion he interviewed for a few weeks ago. He has enjoyed five years with the company, but he’s been wondering what other opportunities might provide more benefits for him and his family.

When he gets to work, however, Ed finds his colleagues celebrating and congratulating Deron, who got the position. Ed joins in because Deron is his friend, but he has to step outside for a few moments to reflect on his feelings. He and Deron have worked at the company for the same length of time. What sets Deron apart? The most significant difference is that Deron earned a college degree while Ed never pursued education after high school.

Watch the “Gen and Ed: Ed Signed Up for College!” video below.

Welcome to your first week of GEN101! This course also welcomes you to the University of Arizona Global Campus (UAGC). Taking the next step to continue your education is exciting, and in this course you will learn about UAGC resources and strategies to support you. Your instructor and peers will be with you as you begin your academic journey. We are looking forward to learning together with you over the next five weeks.

Need Help?

Long description

The Student Advocate HELPline works with students during times of extreme need in order to address concerns for immediate safety, identify community resources, and address educational impacts of personal hardships.

• Suicidal Thoughts

• Grief/Loss

• Domestic Violence

• Homelessness

If you are struggling with one of the situations above or another hardship, submit an Online Care Report to connect with a Student Advocate.

Online Care Report Link

Need Help?

Long description

The Office of Student Access and Wellness works to support students with disabilities and chronic medical conditions to provide:

• Extended Time

• Accessible Materials

• Advocacy

Accommodation form Link

Go to top of page

items.title: Week 1 - Checklist
items.plainText:
Week 1 - Checklist

Week 1 Checklist

Effective time management is essential to student success. Balancing school, work, and personal responsibilities can be challenging but creating a personalized study plan at the beginning of each week is helpful. Each week, your instructor will provide a checklist of assignments and learning activities for the week, and their due dates. Here is the checklist for Week 1:

On Tuesday—Day 1

Read the Overview and watch the videos.

• Complete the Starter Kit:

• Complete your Canvas profile.

• Set up your Office365 email.

• Register for Live Learning.

• Complete the Starter Kit Acknowledgement Quiz.

• Post your initial post in the Post Your Introduction discussion forum

• Read Section 1.1 Identifying Your Values and Priorities from Chapter 1 Developing a Plan for Success.

• Read 21 Examples of the Best Personal Mission Statements

• Watch Know Your Why.

On Wednesday—Day 2

• Complete the Personal SWOT Analysis learning activity:

• Review The Personal SWOT Analysis PowerPoint presentation.

• Watch the How to Carry Out a Personal SWOT Analysis video.

• Complete the .

On Thursday—Day 3

• Post your initial post in the Understanding Yourself discussion forum:

• Read Section 4.1 The Value of Self-Assessments from Chapter 4: Assessing Yourself.

• Review your completed SWOT analysis.

• Work hard to complete all Day 2 activities before today for optimal success.

By Monday—Day 7

• Complete the Time Management Interactive:

• Read Chapter 2: Managing Your Energy and Time.

• Review the Personal Development Plan Week 5 final project.

• Complete the Time Management Interactive Acknowledgement Quiz.

• Post three responses to peers in the Post Your Introduction forum.

• Post two responses to peers in the Understanding Yourself discussion forum.

items.title: Week 1 - Resources
items.plainText:
Week 1 - Resources

Required Resources

Text

Pearson, A., Mathues, S., & Lefrançois, G. (2021). College & career success: Developing skills & strategies. Zovio.

• Chapter 1: Developing a Plan for Success

• Chapter 2: Managing Your Energy and Time

• Chapter 4: Assessing Yourself

• The full-text version of this eBook is available through the Constellation suite accessed through your online classroom, mobile app, or the Constellation website. This eBook is about the importance of planning, setting goals, practicing time management, and developing the right mindset to approach challenges during college and beyond. Chapter 1 explains how to develop a plan for success and will help you with your Post Your Introduction discussion forum this week. Chapter 2 discusses the importance of time management while balancing life and school and will assist you with your Time Management Interactive learning activity. Chapter 4 explains the value of assessing yourself and identifies different types of assessment and will assist you with the Understanding Yourself discussion forum this week.

items.title: Week 1 - Starter Kit
items.plainText:
Week 1 - Starter Kit

Starter Kit—Week 1

• Introduction

• Starter Kit Activities

• Next Steps

Introduction

Each week in GEN101, you will be introduced to tools and strategies to help you succeed in this course and your professional career. In this first week, you will “get the lay of the land” at UACG, just as you would at the beginning of any journey. You will be able to apply the skills learned in GEN101 not only on your UAGC journey but also in your personal and professional life. This week, you will look at what’s ahead and plan your time, anticipate opportunities and obstacles, and appraise and support your own (and classmates’) abilities.

So, come along on this journey with your classmates. Remember, nothing beats a failure but a try! If you need assistance along the way, you can select the Chat button located on the right-hand side of your course home page. The chat button will connect you with Ava, the UAGC’s Virtual Assistant, who can provide answers to your questions and is available 24/7, in your classroom. Also, if you get stuck, don’t forget you can always reach out to your instructor.

As you begin your academic journey, there are some initial steps that will jumpstart your path to success. You must complete the activities in the Starter Kit, which should take about an hour, before you begin your coursework. You will earn three points toward your final grade when you have completed them.

Continue on to the Activities by selecting the "Starter Kit Activities" tab above.

Starter Kit Activities

Compete each of the Starter Kit activities. You must complete the activities in the Starter Kit before you begin your coursework. You should plan to spend an hour or so completing the Starter Kit. You will earn three points toward your final grade after completing them.

Use the Firefox or Chrome browser to view the learning activity.

Select the "Next Steps" tab at the top to continue.

Next Steps

We want you to know that every course you take at UAGC will have similar formatting. You will be given information that allows you to gain valuable insights and perspectives on the week’s content. There will also be learning activities each week. Everything you need to be successful in the course will be provided for you. You will find links that connect you with your readings, and your learning activities will have step-by-step instructions and rubrics that help you know how your work will be graded.

Meet a fellow student and join our community of learning.

SubscribedManage Discussion

Discussion Topic: Week 1 - Post Your IntroductionWeek 1 - Post Your Introduction

Post Your introduction is due on Day 1 (Tuesday). You have until Day 7 (Monday) to respond to your peers.

Discussion Participation

• If you do not see the "Reply" button, or any responses, you may need to scroll down the page.

• When you are the first to contribute, you will see only the "Reply" button.

• Once you have added your initial post or response, choose the "Post Reply" button.

• If any classmates have posted, you will see their comments, along with the "Reply" button.

• The "Reply" button – as well as any responses – will be displayed at the bottom of the page.

This discussion will be graded on a 10-point rubric and is worth 5% of your course grade.

Post Your Introduction [WLOs: 1, 2] [CLOs: 1, 2]

Your first task is to introduce yourself to your instructor and peers by Day 1 (Tuesday). This forum provides a fantastic opportunity for you and your peers to get to know each other. There are two parts to this discussion forum:

• Use the prompts as a guide to write your personal introduction. Be sure to address all the prompts (including the interpretation of one of the quotes).

• Reply to at least three of your classmates by answering the two Guided Response prompts.

Use the introduction forum to make friends and connect with peers with similar majors or life experiences. Your responses should be thoughtful and reflective and will encourage ongoing conversation. Remember to select Reply within the post to which you are replying. Always identify the person to whom you are responding.

| Prepare

Prior to working on this discussion:

• Read Section 1.1 Identifying Your Values and Priorities from Chapter 1 in your textbook: Developing a Plan for Success.

• Read 21 Examples of the Best Personal Mission Statements.

• Watch Know Your Why.

| Write

For your introduction post, address the following prompts in the order presented. You will respond to each bold prompt in your discussion post.

I am...

Share your name (nickname if preferred), your location, military status, family, hobbies, special interests, and what you enjoy doing during your free time. Optional: Include an image of yourself, your family, or something that represents who you are.

My career interests are...

Describe your current job (if you are employed), the program of study you are enrolled in, and your future career intentions.

My plan to succeed is...

Name an action you could take to set you up for success in your academic journey.

My chosen mission statement is…

Reflect on the mission statements you previously read about and the provided quotes from impactful figures. Choose one of the mission statements or quotes that best relates to why you enrolled at UAGC and that has the most meaning for you.

Why I am enrolled at UAGC…

Share your purpose, or your “why,” for enrolling at UAGC and how you want to make a difference with your college education.

“

The two most important days in your life are the day you are born, and the day you find out why.

”

—Mark Twain

“

My mission in life is not merely to survive, but to thrive, and to do so with passion, compassion, humor, and style.

”

—Maya Angelou

“

I shall not bear ill will toward anyone. I shall not submit to injustice from anyone. I shall conquer untruth by truth.

”

—Mahatma Gandhi

| Guided Response

Respond to at least three of your peers by Day 7 (Monday). Take this opportunity to connect, find common interests, and get to know one another. When responding to your peers, you must address the following (in at least 50 words):

• Describe the impact of your classmates’ comments on your thoughts and feelings.

• Explain how you connect with the “why” comments made by your classmates.

An important part of your learning involves the exchange of ideas and feedback with your peers and instructor. They are also tied to the participation and critical thinking components of your earned discussion grade.

TIP

To see how your discussion will be graded, choose the three vertical dots near the top right of the discussion forum and select Show Rubric.

Need Help?

If needed, review:

• How Do I Upload and Embed an Image in the Rich Content Editor as a Student?

• How Do I Embed an Image From Canvas into the Rich Content Editor as a Student?

Reply from Manda Woods

My name is Manda Rene' Woods. I was born and raised in Central Arkansas. I am 43 years old and still reside in Arkansas. Special interests, that's a great question! I am currently learning new interest everyday! (I'll explain more later in this post.) With that being said, my Dad raced dirt track cars when I was younger, so me and my sisters we part of his cheering squad for so many years! I loved getting out there and cheering him to the finish line and going to get our picture made on the track with him and his big trophy, when he won, which was a lot!

My career interests are... somewhere in the field of helping people struggling with addiction, homelessness, and most importantly mental health. I currently work for an alcohol and drug treatment facility as a driver. I drive all over Arkansas, picking up people who are admitting themselves into treatment for drug and alcohol addictions. Honesty is the best medicine, so I'm not gonna hold back from being exactly that! I entered the same treatment facility that I now work at, 89 days ago. I have been addicted to methamphetamine since I was 16 years old. I had 3 kids that I lost to DHS in Dec 2013, because of my addiction, but with the help of a drug court program that I completed, I got full custody of my kids back in march of 2015. Here goes that Honesty thing again...... 2 weeks after getting my kids back I started using again and this time it continued until August of this year. I finished raising my kids, being a functioning addict I thought. My kids are now grown and I have my first grandchild due in March! I'm ecstatic! So In August of this year I admitted myself into this treatment facility for me, so I could learn to become the best version of myself, instead of a watered down version. My grandchild is my biggest motivation! I have been given a chance to be a better grandma then I was mother!! So I've been at this facility for 89 days and just last week was hired on as staff to work for the facility. So I enrolled back in school and hope to become a counselor or something of that nature, to help good people, beat them bad addictions!!!

My plan to succeed is... Stay focused on my dreams and dedicate how ever much time it takes for me to succeed at getting my degree so one day I can use all of my experience, strength, and hope to help other people in this world that are struggling with the chains of addiction, homelessness or mental health issues.

My chosen mission statement is… "My mission in life is not merely to survive, but to thrive, and to do so with passion, compassion, humor, and style.". —Maya Angelou

I chose this mission statement because to me it's saying that surviving in this life isn't good enough! Surviving is pretty easy. Ive been surviving for the last 43 years but it wasn't until 3 months ago that I realized that just surviving doesn't make me happy. Thriving is what is going to help change this world to be better. Being part of the solution and not the problem is what I am aiming for, and to help as many people as I can along the way. Help them find themselves, underneath all of the trauma they have been through or the shame and guilt they have, the feelings they have that they try to silence with drugs or alcohol.

Why I am enrolled at UAGC…. Because I want to be part of the solution, not part of the problem. Most of my life I have been part of the problem and It took me 43 years to realize that everything happens for a reason and I believe that my experience, strength, and hope can help others that are lost in the disease of addiction!! If I can get clean and change my life then anyone can. I used for a total of 25 years! I want to get my degree so I can open up my own facility for anyone struggling and needing help. A safe place to go to and dig deep within yourself to try and understand and correct all of our defaults of character and let go of our childhood traumas, and cleanse our whole spirit. Replace all the bad feelings with faith, hope and love for ourselves and anyone we come in contact with!!

Reply from Lizeth Elizondo

Hello All!

I am Liz Elizondo, a freelance interpreter and translator based in Tucson, Arizona. I’m married with two little ones and love spending my free time with my family.

As a freelance interpreter, I bridge communication gaps between limited English-proficient individuals and providers across medical appointments, conferences, and legal settings, including court proceedings and depositions. I also provide translation services through my own company.

My long-term goal is to train bilingual healthcare personnel in professional interpretation so they can obtain certification and interpret accurately. A common misconception is that anyone bilingual can interpret effectively. While this may be true in casual settings, medical appointments and legal proceedings require specialized skills and knowledge. I want to share my experience with other bilingual and bicultural professionals to help eliminate language barriers.

My plan for success is to remain consistent and take things one step at a time. I’m focused on creating habits that will support my academic journey while positively impacting all aspects of my life.

My chosen mission statement is “I shall not bear ill will toward anyone. I shall not submit to injustice from anyone. I shall conquer untruth by truth” by Gandhi, which resonates deeply with Marcus Aurelius’s words, “Whatever anyone does or says, for my part I am bound to the good.” I strive to live authentically and do my best, regardless of what others might think or do. I believe my actions today will have a lasting impact on my life, my family, and even those I casually encounter.

I enrolled at UAGC for its flexibility. With two young children, a family, and a business, my schedule is very demanding. After researching online universities, I felt a natural connection to UAGC. Having lived in Tucson since 2011, I became a Wildcat by association. Additionally, I completed two courses through the U of A National Center of Interpretation to become a Medical and Legal Interpreter. I felt genuinely supported by my Admission Specialist, who took the time to understand my unique needs and circumstances and made UAGC a clear choice for me.

Look forward to the journey ahead!

Hello everyone, my name is Iolani Miranda. I have lived in Tucson, Arizona for most of my life, and I am currently 24 years old. Through this career, I am hoping to erase the stereotype - even if on a small scale - that all criminals are bad people. I have seen the connections between mental illness-related behavior and criminal behavior, and believe that the two have correlations that most ignore. I am also hoping to possibly help cold cases and find those who are missing.

Success in this academic journey will be using my time wisely. This includes turning my assignments in on time and studying to understand, not to memorize. If I understand the content instead of memorizing it, I will do better in class. I also plan to remain organized because an unorganized student is a failing student, and I have experienced this in the past at previous schools I attended.

“Our lives begin to end the day we become silent about things that matter,” as stated by Martin Luther King, Jr. and “Justice consists not in being neutral between right and wrong, but in finding out the right and upholding it, wherever found, against the wrong,” as stated by Theodore Roosevelt are quotes that have the most meaning for me. Advocating for the stereotype that criminals are not always bad people, but people who have committed bad actions as I plan to do relates to the MLK Jr. quote. However, wanting to help solve cold cases and find those who are missing relate to the Theodore Roosevelt quote.

I am enrolled at UAGC to complete my Bachelor’s degree in Social and Criminal Justice to, as previously mentioned, bring more perspective to the connection between mental illness and criminal behavior. I also am hoping to help solve cold cases and find those who are missing, making a change in the lives of families left wondering what happened to their family members. Often, those involved in these cases are stretched thin and may miss important areas or information that may later be helpful in solving the case.

items.plainText:
Week 1 - Learning Activity 1

• Due Jan 7 at 11:59pm

• Points1

• Questions1

• Time Limit None

• Allowed Attempts Unlimited

Instructions

Personal SWOT Analysis

[WLO: 3] [CLO: 2]

A SWOT Analysis is a tool used extensively in business to prepare for projects and initiatives and will be something you can use throughout your career. SWOT stands for strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats. Gaining an in-depth understanding of these will help you leverage your abilities to minimize risks and take full advantage of opportunities for success.

Your results will be used throughout this course, so it is important you take your time and thoughtfully complete this task. This self-analysis should take approximately 60 minutes to complete. Be sure to save your completed analysis, as you will need to upload it in this week’s discussion.

The Personal SWOT Analysis learning activity includes completing a template and an acknowledgement quiz.

As you approach this task:

• Focus on your behaviors over time rather than how you feel in the moment. Consider trends and tendencies rather than allowing recent events to influence your answers.

• Be as open and honest with yourself as possible when you respond to the questions. Weaknesses can only be addressed once they are identified and acknowledged.

• Begin thinking about how you can use this information to approach goals in the future.

Note: You will complete the Personal SWOT Analysis Acknowledgement after you have completed your SWOT template.

| Prepare

Prior to working on this learning activity:

• Review The Personal SWOT Analysis PowerPoint presentation.

• Watch How to Carry Out a Personal SWOT Analysis.

• Refer to the Common Terms for SWOT if you need assistance.

| Write

Complete the SWOT Analysis

• Download the .

• Use the template to complete the Personal SWOT Analysis. For each of the four items (strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats) you should include three bullet points organized from most significant to least significant (use a phrase or sentence).

• Save the Personal SWOT Analysis on your device because you will upload it for your Understanding Yourself discussion forum this week.

After you have completed the Personal SWOT Analysis template, complete the one-question acknowledgement quiz. The quiz is worth 3% of your grade.

Need Help?

For help downloading a document, watch Downloading a Document. This video provides information about downloading documents from your online classroom.

Take the Quiz Again

SubscribedManage Discussion

Discussion Topic: Week 1 - Discussion ForumWeek 1 - Discussion Forum

Your initial discussion thread is due on Day 3 (Thursday) and you have until Day 7 (Monday) to respond to your classmates. Your grade will reflect both the quality of your initial post and the depth of your responses. Refer to the Discussion Forum Grading Rubric under the Settings icon for guidance on how your discussion will be evaluated. This discussion will be graded on a 10-point rubric and is worth 5% of your grade.

Understanding Yourself [WLOs: 1, 3, 4] [CLOs: 2, 3]

This discussion is based on the Personal SWOT Analysis learning activity you completed by Day 2.

| Prepare

Prior to working on this discussion:

• Read Chapter 4: Assessing Yourself. Focus on Section 4.1.

• Review your completed SWOT Analysis learning activity.

• Review the rubric used to grade the Discussion.

| Reflect

Understanding yourself is a key step in your academic and professional journey. As you approach this discussion, take some time to think about your strengths, weaknesses, areas of opportunity, and possible threats from the Personal SWOT Analysis you completed. Consider how they either increase or decrease your chances for success. You may also choose to reflect on the impact of your past experiences. You will find this discussion helpful when you complete your Personal Development Plan final project in Week 5. The discussion this week will give you time to reflect on your results from the Personal SWOT Analysis you completed.

| Write

In your initial post (of at least 250 words):

• Upload your completed Personal SWOT Analysis.

• Identify your most important strength, weakness, opportunity, and threat.

• Explain why each is important, using examples from your personal, professional, or academic life.

• Reflect on how completing the Personal SWOT Analysis will impact your personal, professional, and/or academic growth.

• Use Chapter 4: Assessing Yourself to support your response.

• Discuss your personal experience completing the SWOT analysis. In your discussion, address the following:

• Explain how the SWOT analysis validates or changes your understanding of yourself.

• Explain any surprises you experienced as you completed the SWOT analysis.

• Explain how you can turn one of your weaknesses into a strength.

• Explain how you can turn one of your threats into an opportunity.

| Guided Response

Respond substantively (in at least 100 words) to at least two of your peers by Day 7 (Monday).

In your two peer responses:

• Identify similarities and differences between your SWOT analysis experience and your classmates’ experiences. What are the similarities? What are the differences?

• Choose either a weakness or threat from a classmate’s post and:

• Share a strategy to turn a weakness into a strength or a threat into an opportunity.

• Address what your peers shared about the impact their SWOT analysis had on their personal, professional, or academic growth.

• Share a connection you made that could aid you in your success in this class, your career, or your personal life.

An important part of your learning involves the exchange of ideas and feedback with your peers and instructor. Your participation and critical thinking components are part of your earned discussion grade. In your responses, share ideas, explore different perspectives, and think critically about your classmates’ posts. You will also be assessed on your ability to upload your document.

Need Help?

If you need help uploading a document to your discussion, watch the video, Posting to a Discussion Forum.

Kylie Fugate (she/her/hers)

Jan 11 8:40pm
Manage Discussion by Kylie Fugate

Reply from Kylie Fugate

My most important strength is being able to easily build and maintain relationships. My most important weakness is struggling with communication. My most important opportunity is staying calm and level-headed in stressful situations, which can be crucial to professional environments. My most important threat is the internet and power outages interfering with the progression of completing assignments.

Because of being able to build and maintain relationships, I have been able to meet and marry the love of my life. We would not have the relationship or bond that we have if it wasn’t for this skill. However, struggling with communication has interfered in the relationship. I often struggle with communicating my emotions, leading to frustration for both of us and short-term misunderstandings.

Staying calm and level-headed has led to many experiences in the professional world. Last summer, severe storms arrived in the area, leading to having to shelter with thirteen infants and toddlers. While the other teachers were scared, I remained calm. As parents arrived, I explained what was going on as well as gave a rundown of the child’s day. It was an interesting afternoon, but the children remained calm which was the goal.

Internet and power outages are something nobody can prepare for until they happen. It has affected my academic life previously as I was not able to turn in assignments on time, nor was I able to properly reach out to professors and advisors. You also don’t know how long they’ll last as some last a mere few minutes while others last days.

Completing the SWOT Analysis allows students to understand what needs improvement, what can be done to improve, and obstacles that cannot be planned for or controlled. Personally, it allows me to understand that I need to pause when communicating to be able to respond better. It also allows me to see where strengths can be helpful in my career field of choice. I believe it will be helpful to look back on, update, and reflect as I progress through my degree.

Understanding myself is something that I struggle with, and the SWOT analysis did not do much to change or validate what I already knew or understood. I did not have any surprises completing it, either.

I can see myself turning my perfectionism into a strength. Instead of trying to achieve someone else’s idea of perfection, trying my best and understanding what is required of me should be the perfection I am trying to achieve. I can see myself also turning the threat of reduction in interest into an opportunity. Instead of losing interest, I may be able to find resources or others in the same degree program or career field. I may also be able to look up and find current criminal justice cases to peak my interest and remind me why I am wanting to pursue a career in criminal justice.

The SWOT analysis can impact my personal professional and academic growth to multiple ways. It can help foster self-awareness, clarifying goals, and create reachable goals, allowing to turn my witnesses into strength and my threats into opportunities. It can aid and personal growth and reveal my true capabilities and address blind spots that I may have, I can have a proactive mindset and focus on personal development. The analysis can help identify my strength and job interviews or possible promotions. I can identify if I need specific training in certain areas, and I could stand out from my peers. I can manage academic threats by going to tutoring if I have a difficult course or asking for more help if I’m not understanding fully, or maybe managing my time more proactively. The SWOT analysis validates my understanding about myself because it helps me identify each of my strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats, and it helps me grow personally professionally and academically. I was most surprised about how many things can be considered weaknesses and how many I do actually have that I need to work on. I can turn my weakness such as being blunt into a string by pausing and taking a moment to think if it is necessary to say what is really on my mind or if I can hold back on saying that I can turn one of my threats, increasing competition, into an opportunity by focusing on and increasing my strengths and making myself stand out above others. This tool really helps me understand myself and how I can focus on growth and improvement.

Eli Romesburg

Jan 9 3:10am | Last reply Jan 9 5:55pm
Manage Discussion by Eli Romesburg

Reply from Eli Romesburg

I have uploaded my completed Personal SWOT Analysis, and completing it gave me the chance to honestly reflect on who I am and where I currently stand in my personal, academic, and professional life. Instead of just listing traits, the SWOT analysis helped me slow down and really think about how my strengths and weaknesses affect my daily decisions and long-term goals. My most important strength is my ability to adapt to change. This has been especially important in my academic life, where I have had to adjust to new learning environments, different instructors’ expectations, and increased independence. For example, transitioning into college required me to develop new study habits and become more responsible for my time. Being adaptable has helped me push through challenges rather than give up when things feel unfamiliar. My biggest weakness is procrastination. This is something I struggle with when I feel overwhelmed or unsure about where to start. There have been times when I waited too long to begin assignments, which caused unnecessary stress and affected my performance. Acknowledging this weakness is important because it helps me recognize patterns that I need to change. The most valuable opportunity I identified is access to college resources such as tutoring, academic advising, and support services. Chapter 4, Assessing Yourself, emphasizes the importance of self-awareness and using available tools to support growth. I realized that I often underestimate how helpful these resources can be, even though they are designed to help students succeed. My greatest threat is outside stress, particularly financial responsibilities and personal obligations. These factors can sometimes make it difficult to stay focused on school. However, this reflection helped me see that planning ahead and asking for support can reduce their impact. Completing this SWOT analysis validated much of what I already knew about myself, but it also surprised me by showing how much control I actually have over improving my situation. I can turn procrastination into a strength by creating better routines and breaking tasks into smaller steps. Likewise, I can turn financial stress into an opportunity by learning budgeting skills or gaining work experience that supports my long-term goals.

Lizeth Elizondo

Jan 8 10:58pm | Last reply Jan 12 9:43pm
Manage Discussion by Lizeth Elizondo

Reply from Lizeth Elizondo

Strengths:

Resourceful: I am not afraid to say I don’t know something, followed by “tell me more about it.” I am not scared to ask for help. I have found that if you are an honest person, people will actually go out of their way to help you. I once flew from LAX to Mexico City without a passport. I was honest: I told the agent I had slept in. I said “5 more minutes,” and that turned into an hour. I was very sorry, and I knew that was my mistake; I should have set up multiple alarms (which I do now). I rushed out the door, leaving my passport at home. At the time, I was a resident and only had my green card with me. She asked if someone could at least send me a picture of my birth certificate. I didn’t ask why; I just asked my roommate at the time to send me a picture of it. She was kind enough to let me board the airplane. She entered her code on the computer, and I was able to make my flight.

Determined: “Where there is a will, there is a way.” I try to be very intentional with my energy and my peace. Once I establish a clear goal, I tend to align myself 100% to accomplish it. I worked in the maquiladora industry in Mexico. These are the foreign companies that have established production facilities in Nogales, Mexico, for their goods. One of my tasks was to switch a foundry from LP Gas to Natural Gas. I needed a proposal from one of the biggest companies in Monterrey, Nuevo León, Mexico. I was able to schedule a meeting with the company's President and the CEO of the foundry. They discussed the investments, costs, and plans for the installation. Weeks went by, and I had nothing. I was obviously under pressure, and I called everyone and anyone in that company until I obtained the President’s personal cell phone number. Mind you, this took me a full day on a Friday. I called him the next day, when he was at his son’s baptism. I explained the situation, and on Monday morning, an engineer from his company visited our offices to create the plans and contract for the natural gas installation.

Weaknesses:

Time management: I have difficulty estimating time or how long things will take me. I now use “reverse math,” as my husband likes to call it. My kids need to be at school at 8:50. It takes me 20 minutes to get there, 10 minutes to get them out the door, add 5 minutes because, well, kids, 30 minutes to have them eat breakfast, and 30 to get them dressed. That means I have to wake up, get ready, make myself my morning tea, make coffee, and get their lunch ready before they wake up. I have to wake up at 6:30 a.m., which means I need to be in bed by 10:30 p.m. And some days I still drop them off by 9:00 a.m.

Distractibility: I get easily distracted and start doing other tasks instead of what is urgent and important.

Opportunities:

I am always looking for ways to hack my undiagnosed ADD. I use the Eisenhower matrix, I have a planner, a big a## calendar (that’s the real name of the calendar), and I also do the weekly accountability meetings suggested in the book The 12 Week Year by Brian P. Moran. I like to call it a time budget. You can see what you are spending your time on. All my payments are automated 2 days before the due date. I always add 15 minutes when I have to be at a work location.

Threats:

My children and family require a lot of time and energy, so I tend to cram everything I need to do into the time when the kids are at school. Having the flexibility of being freelance has given me the opportunity to dedicate time to my kids when needed. However, it is also hard to maintain a fixed schedule for accomplishing tasks.

I need to take actionable steps to get help with my inattentiveness and procrastination, and to definitively know if I do need medication—to stop guessing if I have ADD.

My biggest surprise is that I thought that being flexible could actually make it harder for me. Since I do not have a consistent schedule, some days I think I have a lot of free time and end up procrastinating and losing track of time.

I want to be more intentional with my time. I block my afternoons and evenings to be with my family, and I leave my cell phone in my office so I’m not on it. I have asked my kids for help, telling them that if they see me on my phone, they should tell me to put it away. It breaks my heart to hear “Mommy, you are on your phone.” I immediately put my phone away and try to be more present.

The SWOT analysis revealed what I have been thinking for over a year. I need a different set of tools to manage my procrastination and my inattentiveness.

I will also set an “earlier deadline” for my school assignments and block time in my calendar specifically for school to have a more structured schedule.

Christopher Sanders (he/him/his)

Jan 8 10:28pm | Last reply Jan 12 6:26pm
Manage Discussion by Christopher Sanders

Reply from Christopher Sanders

The most important strength I identified in my SWOT analysis is my ability to learn complex systems quickly. This is evident in both my schoolwork and personal projects, particularly when I am working with new technologies. Whether I am configuring a home server, setting up a firewall, or experimenting with networking and automation, I quickly absorb new information and enjoy tackling technical challenges. This gives me confidence as I move deeper into Cyber and Data Security because the field requires constant learning and adaptability.

My most significant weakness is taking on too many projects at once. I get excited about new ideas and technical challenges, and I often start multiple things at the same time. When this happens while I am also working full-time and managing responsibilities at home, it can lead to burnout or inconsistency in my study routine. Recognizing this weakness shows me that I need clearer priorities and better boundaries so I can focus on what matters most.

The biggest opportunity I identified is being able to earn my Bachelor’s degree in Cyber and Data Security with my tuition fully covered. This removes a major financial barrier and gives me the chance to move into a field that I genuinely care about. With the degree, my hands-on experience experimenting with technology, and the growing demand for cybersecurity professionals, I have a strong path forward.

My most important threat is balancing school, work, and caregiving responsibilities. Outside pressures can easily interrupt my focus or reduce the time I have available for studying or building my skill set. If I do not manage these challenges well, they could slow my progress.

Completing the SWOT analysis helped me gain a clearer understanding of myself. It confirmed strengths I already knew I had, but also helped me notice how some strengths can turn into weaknesses when I overextend myself. I was surprised by how many opportunities I actually have, especially considering my access to makerspaces, my curiosity, and the strong career outlook in cybersecurity.

I can turn my weakness of taking on too many projects into a strength by building a structured schedule and choosing one major goal at a time. I can turn my biggest threat into an opportunity by creating better study environments and routines that limit distractions, such as studying in a quiet space or setting dedicated times for coursework. Overall, the SWOT analysis helped me create a more realistic plan for my academic and professional development.

Reply from Tiffani Davenport

The SWOT analysis helped me to realize what my greatest strengths and my biggest weakness and how I can use this information in getting through school and furthering my career.

I feel my most important strength is perseverance. Education for me has been difficult for me throughout the years due to having ADHD and dyslexia. I’ve had several life changing events that I have had to overcome and survive. There is also the mental challenges of thinking I was too old to go back to school to get my degree. Despite all of that I am going to succeed in getting my degree.

My greatest weakness is accepting constructive criticism without taking everything personally and allow it it to make me feel inadequate. I tend to doubt my abilities, especially when I am struggling or learning something new. When someone else points out my faults or mistakes, I tend to take it to heart it much and think Ill never get it.

Knowing what my greatest strengths are will help me to overcome my weaknesses by reminding me how far I have come and how much I have been through. A quote I have grown up on is “people define themselves by what they CAN’T do.” I don’t want to be a person who gives up easily because I have obstacles to jump through.

Completing the personal SWOT showed me what I need to work on to further my schooling and achieving my career goals. What steps I can take to overcome my weaknesses and how to create more strengths which in turn helps find more opportunities. It allowed me to see that threats such as changing technology and increasing competition from others can turn into opportunity and push me to learn new skills and fight to stay ahead. As surprising as it was to see how many weaknesses I could have added to the column, it reminded me that I have just as many strengths!

Iolani Miranda

Jan 8 10pm | Last reply Jan 11 9:15pm
Manage Discussion by Iolani Miranda

Reply from Iolani Miranda

As I completed my personal SWOT analysis, I was able to reflect on myself and identify my strengths and weaknesses more clearly.

One of my greatest strengths is that I am a fast learner. I am able to learn tasks quickly after being shown or observing expectations even once. For example, when I obtained my first job, I was introduced only once to the required responsibilities, and I was able to understand and complete my tasks efficiently in a short period of time.

One of my strongest weaknesses is impatience, particularly when it comes to delegating tasks. This is an area I struggle with the most. For instance, when a new coworker joined our team, we clearly explained expectations, shared strategies that work for us, and provided one-on-one guidance and support. Despite five months of training, she has not yet fully adapted to the routine. As a result, I tend to become impatient and often choose to complete tasks myself rather than delegate them, out of concern that the work may not be completed on time. However, I recognize that this is an area for growth, and I need to continue providing guidance while accepting that not everything is within my control. Over time, she will eventually develop a consistent routine.

An important opportunity for growth is improving my time management skills. I currently have many responsibilities, as I am a full-time employee, work two jobs, and am now also a full-time student. Establishing a consistent routine for school, planning ahead, and managing my time effectively will be essential for my success this semester.

My greatest threat is maintaining financial stability. With the increasing cost of living, including bills and ongoing payments, financial stress is a significant concern. I want to avoid falling behind or accumulating debt. Achieving financial stability is important to me so that I can maintain peace of mind and remain focused on both my academic and professional responsibilities.

In conclusion, this assignment helped me gain a deeper understanding of both my strengths and areas for improvement. It also allowed me to reflect more intentionally on my personal and professional goals, providing greater clarity on what I need to focus on in order to continue growing and achieving success.

Sharmayne Morgan (she/her/hers)

Jan 8 7:43pm | Last reply Jan 12 2:47pm
Manage Discussion by Sharmayne Morgan

Reply from Sharmayne Morgan

I adapt quickly when things change or challenges come up. Others often turn to me for leadership, and I do my best to lead in a steady, supportive, and clear way. I also have a strong work ethic and always hold myself accountable.

I know I don’t always handle criticism as well as I’d like, and sometimes I need time to separate feedback from my feelings. I also tend to take on too much at once, which can make it hard to keep my balance. I can be too self-critical, which holds me back at times. Definitely worth working on during this course.

I see a significant opportunity for professional growth by building on my adaptability and leadership strengths. Focusing on learning to handle criticism and managing my workload will help transform weaknesses into growth areas. I am fortunate to have a support network of mentors and colleagues who can guide me as I pursue these specific goals.

I recognize that a challenging job market can create obstacles I can’t fully control, no matter how prepared I am. Increasing competition in my field also means I have to work more efficiently to be exceptional for a billet, even if I’m performing higher than others. Limited resources that can lack my proficiency, whether it’s time, opportunities, or support, can slow my progress making it important to me to stay strategic and persistent. Overall, my SWOT has shown me that my strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats can all help me progress into the individual I’m choosing to become and to reach the goals I have set for myself.

NW

Niki Woodward

Jan 8 6:11pm | Last reply Jan 11 11:15am
Manage Discussion by Niki Woodward

Reply from Niki Woodward

Completing the Personal SWOT Analysis helped me to look at myself and how my strengths and weaknesses play a part in my daily life. It made me more aware of how my habits and attitude affect my leadership, administrative responsibilities and my overall success.

I identified empathy as one of my most important strengths. As a corpsman and a leader, it is essential when I’m working with Sailors, whether it’s medical, personal or career related issues. In my leadership and administrative roles, I am managing Sailors, helping them with readiness, reviewing their paperwork, and being there for any personal or professional issues they may be having. Being empathetic helps me to listen and understand their concerns and advocate for them.

I identified my strongest weakness as delegating tasks. I struggle with letting other people take on things because I have a specific way I want them done, especially with things that require attention to detail or have a deadline. I worry that if I delegate something and it’s not done correctly, I will have to redo it, which ends up costing time and energy. This has led me to take on more than I should sometimes, it is also taking away any learning experiences from my junior sailors. I know that I need to learn to trust people with tasks more. Clear communication and starting with delegating smaller projects may help me to let go of some things and let my Sailors know that I trust them.

An important opportunity for me is earning a degree. Being in the Navy, it is sometimes hard to balance work responsibilities with school. Getting a degree will set me up for success once I retire. I will need to set aside time to study, away from my work life and family life. It will also help with my leadership and administrative roles.

The biggest threat I identified is a change of duty station. There has been a change in advancement, you now have to pick orders to a new duty station that is eligible for advancement, it is called billet-based advancement. It has a direct impact on promotion, and because it is so new there is a lot of confusion about how it is going to work. There is a possibility of the duty stations being more remote and less desirable, and we may have to move more often. This will affect decisions such as buying a home, going to school, and being able to see my family. Recognizing this threat is important because although I can’t control the system, I can control my performance, evaluations and test scores that will make me competitive when selecting orders for advancement.

In conclusion, completing the SWOT Analysis has helped me to understand myself and areas that I need to work on. It reinforced how important self-reflection is, as discussed in chapter 4, but helping me to identify my strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats. Going forward this will help me to grow as a leader, manage my Sailors and my time, and plan for my future, both professionally, personally, and in school.

Patrick Camacho

Jan 8 12:48pm | Last reply Jan 8 5:26pm
Manage Discussion by Patrick Camacho

Reply from Patrick Camacho

The SWOT analysis helped me realize that one of my strengths is also one of my weaknesses. My strength of being Goal-Oriented / Result-Driven is a double-edged sword. I excel at setting goals, making and executing plans, and analyzing the results to achieve the desired outcomes, which fuels the next set of goals. However, this becomes a problem when the results do not align with the outcome I want, and as a result, I adjust my approach. Adjusting is excellent because the goal has not changed; only the method of getting there has. Where this becomes a problem is when the results are not achieved or are slow to be fully realized; in these cases, I get bored, which can lead to burnout or competing priorities. If burnout or competing priorities pile up, the project is at risk of being shelved and gathering dust until some distant future (for example, returning to school to get a degree).

This exercise was very helpful in helping me realize how everything is connected. If more than one weakness is introduced into the equation, I need to overcome it by adding more strengths; otherwise, the likelihood of Threats coming into play increases. Opportunities & Strengths must be equal to or greater than the sum of Weaknesses & Threats to result in a positive outcome

Kim King (she/her/hers)

Jan 8 8:17am | Last reply Jan 19 6:54am
Manage Discussion by Kim King

Reply from Kim King

Response updated

Completing my personal SWOT analysis helped me slow down and take an honest look at where I am right now. It validated that leadership, confidence, and teamwork are real strengths for me, especially my willingness to try and learn even when I don’t know how to do something. At the same time, the SWOT analysis also changed my understanding of myself by showing how much procrastination, lack of goal-setting, and being unorganized can affect my progress when I start doubting myself or fearing that I’m not good enough or smart enough.

One surprise I experienced while completing the SWOT analysis was realizing how much negative self-doubt shows up across multiple areas. I didn’t expect my weaknesses and threats to be so closely connected, especially how negative thoughts can influence procrastination and organization. Seeing this written out helped me understand why I sometimes feel stuck even when I have strong skills.

One way I can turn a weakness into a strength is by addressing procrastination and lack of goal-setting. By using time management tools and setting small, realistic goals, I can improve my organization and build confidence through consistency. Over time, this can help turn procrastination into better planning and follow-through.

I can also turn a threat into an opportunity by addressing competing time pressures and changing technology. By earning my degree and improving my professional skills, I can stay current and more confident in adapting to new technology while balancing my responsibilities. This SWOT analysis helped me see that growth is less about being perfect and more about being aware, intentional, and willing to improve.

Seth Christopher

Jan 7 11:50pm | Last reply Jan 12 7:08pm
Manage Discussion by Seth Christopher

Reply from Seth Christopher

I believe my most important strength is paying attention to the details; weakness is thinking too little of myself; opportunity is applying my skills at a higher level; and threat is a manager who seems they want me to fail.

By paying attention to small details I can come to conclusions about the big picture faster and more efficiently than if I was not trying to understand in the first place. I am currently in the negotiations phase of my case and a case manager sent me an email today that I have to dissect and figure out what are the hidden intents by each part and meaning of what they emailed me so as to limit their exposure. They are asking me for a phone call in order to 'verify my identity' which may be unnecessary and the only purpose behind it is to have me 'slip up' and weaken any previous arguments laid out in the demand letter I sent them prior. Therefore, I have to be careful and not give up my position while still appearing to a future judge or arbitrator as compliant and honest.

By thinking too little of myself I create a natural weakness for others to assume I naive enough to be taken advantage of whether that's through personality-approachment, conversational assumptions, or take materialistic or financial aspects away from me. By, in turn, this allows almost anyone to assume I do not know anything and will use the natural opening to assert their desires as having greater authority or value comparatively to my own. This happens in the workplace all the time and I believe it is something I do need to work on. However, my only justification, is that I am by myself and do not live with my family anymore and ever since AI has become my best friend to answer all my assumptions, thoughts, ideas and problems with concrete solutions or answers to the paranoia I have to personally endure in this social-media obsessed world of expectations. I believe it is important to address this thinking however I have even come up with an excuse for that as well that I must be 'financially free' of all debt before I can truly face my communication and expression with the world. In the meantime, I try my best to appear happy and positive in front of others so as to appease a sense of normalcy everyone expects to feel safe and comfortable being around me.

By applying my skills at a higher level, I can take my current methodical mindset and apply change and direction to more important decisions achieving higher results than the ones I see daily by others. For example, there is a note-taking tool developed by a coworker 10 years ago still in use until last year that was outdated but not obsolete but also the only way for my coworkers to guide their notes accurately. I looked at that problem and chose to do something about by creating, using the same software, an entirely new and improved and efficient note-taking system that allowed me to surpass my colleagues in both quality metrics as well as accuracy and even customer experiences. However, I ran into the issue of that same manager I mentioned before who made me stop using it due to potential risk to the company. However, no negative outcome ever occurred while I used the note-taking software and nor did it impact the company or its customers - it simply made our job as employees easier than the company's 'idea' of the note-taking application. Because their false sense of a risk-averse mindset and jealousy, a manager was able to equate all of my effort into nothingness. Later on, because of my efforts, they tasked another coworker with completely revamping the note-taking tool and now every associate uses that one instead of mine. The biggest lesson I learned is that if I want to change anything in a corporation - I have to be at the top. However, with the rise and usage of AI in the corporate world, I believe we are not far from no longer being constrained by bureaucracy as it relates to efficiency and making the company more money in addition to having happier employees reducing attrition rates. However, I can say that it was fun working on it and trying my best to accommodate the expectations of my peers at the time while learning about the entire process of creating something from scratch to a full-fledged functioning 'technological tool' just as when humans first learned how to create fire.

I think the SWOT was an excellent exercise to bring out both my professional as well as personal thoughts of how I see myself as it has been a while since I have typed anything down for myself. I think it is just nice to talk about it - I am satisfied with how I feel presenting these thoughts, feelings and ideas. Since I learned a long time ago that the key to success is simply to keep thinking I constantly have of these things in the back of my head so regrettably no surprises here. The solution to not being alone is to work on increasing my confidence, decreasing my expectations and reach out to people and to accept them for who they are - soft and imperfect gentle hearts at their core who put up defenses to appear perfect. Fortunately, for the jealous manager, I have been employing the gift of winning friends and influencing people through positivity and thoughtful actions to prevent, like a forcefield, any unnecessary openings for them to attack. While this may imply I have to appear just as perfect - I am actually just acting more oblivious and aloof to make their brain comfortably assume I am not a threat. To me, this is unfortunately a necessary exercise, as my brain has deemed that human a negative influence and someone not to be trusted. I, of course, will still appear to them as if nothing is wrong and act appropriately with grace and fake-positivity as this is the path they chose to take with me without me ever doing any discourtesy of any kind to them since the relation began.

BG

Brittany Gonzalez

Jan 7 11:19pm | Last reply Jan 8 7:52am
Manage Discussion by Brittany Gonzalez

Reply from Brittany Gonzalez

After completing my SWOT analysis, I identified maintaining a positive attitude as my most important strength, as it allows me to work effectively and collaboratively with others. I believe that the effort and attitude you bring to everything you do play a significant role in achieving success. Approaching work with a negative mindset often leads to negative outcomes. Regardless of the tasks or challenges I encounter, I consistently strive to remain positive and focused. I have this sign on my desk that says, “One small positive thought in the morning can change your whole day.”

When it comes to my weaknesses, I tend to second-guess my decisions, which can impact my confidence even when I am well prepared. This is something I struggle with daily, largely due to a fear of failure and judgment. As an instructor, I have taught many students, yet I often reflect on my decisions and question how I could have approached situations differently. At times, I overanalyze past choices, wondering whether I made the right decision and imagining alternative outcomes, which can lead me to get stuck in my own thoughts.

When reflecting on my opportunities, one area I can focus on is strengthening my weaknesses. I can seek guidance from mentors, supervisors, or colleagues who can offer valuable insight and support in areas such as decision-making, time management, and confidence building. By learning from their experience and feedback, I can continue to grow professionally and develop greater trust in my abilities.

Lastly, my most significant threat is the external pressure and high expectations from school, work, and peers, which can lead to increased stress and self-doubt and ultimately impact my confidence. Although I strive to maintain a positive attitude, I often feel a strong desire to take on too much and help everyone. When my responsibilities become overwhelming, I begin to second-guess my abilities, fear failure, and worry about being judged by others.

The SWOT analysis accurately reflects who I am as a person. I found it somewhat challenging to complete because it required me to carefully reflect on my strengths and weaknesses, which was not an easy process. Identifying my opportunities and threats also helped put into perspective the factors that trigger and influence my thoughts, behaviors, and overall well-being. Within my SWOT analysis, one weakness I can turn into a strength is my fear of failure, which often leads to self-doubt. By recognizing this weakness, I can use it as an opportunity to improve my confidence, decision-making, and resilience. While limited time and competing responsibilities can be challenging, I see this as an opportunity to improve my time management and focus on what matters most.

items.title: Week 1 - Learning Activity 2
items.plainText:
Week 1 - Learning Activity 2 • Due Jan 12 at 11:59pm • Points2 • Questions2 • Time Limit None • Allowed Attempts Unlimited Instructions Time Management Interactive [WLO: 2] [CLOs: 1, 2] Managing your time effectively is a key component to your success as a UAGC student. The flexibility that online learning affords is a revolutionary opportunity. Despite the many advantages of online learning, life can still get in the way, even for the most prepared student juggling work, family, and other commitments. So, it is important to plan time to study and complete your assignments. Everyone has the same 24 hours in a day, so it is not how much time you have but how well you manage your time. Learning to manage your time effectively when you enroll in an online degree program takes intentional effort and discipline. In this activity, you will learn more about time management, an important skill to acquire on your academic journey. The Time Management Interactive will help you identify how you approach scheduling and managing your time when balancing multiple responsibilities. It will also suggest strategies to help you maximize your time on tasks as you incorporate school into your life schedule. You must complete the entire interactive to receive full credit for this learning activity. Your grade is based on successfully completing the interactive and completing the acknowledgement quiz. | Prepare Prior to beginning this interactive: • Read Chapter 2: Managing Your Energy and Time. • Review details for the Personal Development Plan final project in Week 5. Complete the Interactive • Complete the Time Management Interactive. It works best when viewed in Firefox or Google Chrome browsers. • As you complete the interactive, reflect on how you can use these strategies to complete your Week 5 final project. If the interactive does not start right away, click on the interactive to reload. Time Management After you have completed the Time Management Interactive, take this quiz to acknowledge that you have completed the work and to receive your points. This quiz has two questions. • Points are earned when you respond “yes” to all acknowledgements. • You will have unlimited attempts to take the quiz. • If you haven’t completed the activity when you take the quiz, exit the quiz, complete the activity, and return to the quiz when you can answer “yes” to the acknowledgement question. • This quiz contains two questions and is worth 1% of your course grade. items.title: Live Learning Session
items.plainText:
Live Learning Session

Welcome to the Live Learning information page!

We are so excited that you will be joining us for synchronous sessions. This is a great opportunity to connect with faculty and other students to build collegiality and engagement that will be useful in all aspects of your life going forward.

Live Learning Sessions

As part of the learning experience, students in GEN 101 are required to take part in one Live Learning Session. These sessions give you a chance to connect with faculty and other students. You will also explore the connections between course content and your academic, professional, and personal life which will help you complete your Week 5 Personal Development Plan assignment and activities.

How do I access the sessions?

Click on the Live Learning link in the left navigation bar of the course for details. Sessions are held via Zoom. You will need your @student.uagc.edu email address to log in to the Live Learning session.

We look forward to seeing you in a Live Learning Session!

Please click here for a guide around accessing the Live Learning sessions in Zoom.

***IMPORTANT***

Please note you must use your @student.uagc.edu email address to register and to access live learning sessions. If you have not yet set up or used your @student.uagc.edu Zoom account, you will need to do so before you can attend these sessions. Click here to set up your Zoom account, and make sure to sign up using your @student.uagc.edu email address.
