Weekly Learning Outcomes

This week you will

• Discuss competencies for career success.

• Identify resources to enhance professional growth.

• Identify resources in the Student Success Center.

• Identify strategies, tools, and resources that enhance academic growth.

Introduction

Welcome to your second week of GEN101!

Remember, each week in GEN101, you will be introduced to new tools and strategies to help you succeed in this course, your educational voyage, and your professional career. This week, you will learn more about the free UAGC student resources available on your journey to your college degree. You will also practice the skills you need to complete assignments in this class and future classes.

During GEN101, you are learning strategies and skills you can apply to classes and to your personal and your professional life. This week, you will,

• Reflect on your support network.

• Plan for opportunities for personal development.

• Learn about UAGC Student Support Center services.

|

Need Help?

Long description

The Student Advocate HELPline works with students during times of extreme need in order to address concerns for immediate safety, identify community resources, and address educational impacts of personal hardships.

• Suicidal Thoughts

• Grief/Loss

• Domestic Violence

• Homelessness

If you are struggling with one of the situations above or another hardship, submit an Online Care Report to connect with a Student Advocate.

Online Care Report Link

Need Help?

Long description

The Office of Student Access and Wellness works to support students with disabilities and chronic medical conditions to provide:

• Extended Time

• Accessible Materials

• Advocacy

Accommodation form Link

Go to top of page

items.title: Week 2 - Checklist
items.plainText:
Week 2 - Checklist

Week 2 Checklist

Effective time management is essential to student success. Balancing school, work, and personal responsibilities can be challenging but creating a personalized study plan at the beginning of each week is helpful. Each week, your instructor will provide a checklist of assignments and learning activities for the week, and their due dates. Here is the checklist for Week 2:

On Tuesday—Day 1

Read the Overview and watch the related video

• Complete Week 2 Starter Kit activities

• Read 7.3 and 7.4 of Chapter 7: Learning and Coursework Strategies.

• Read Chapter 9: Note-Taking Strategies.

• Review Downloading a Document to learn how to download and save a file/template to your computer.

• Read Submitting Assignments Through Waypoint.

• Watch Introduction to Waypoint and Originality Checks.

• Watch Accessing Your Waypoint Feedback.

• Read about Grammarly on the Writing Center website.

• Follow the directions to set up your free Grammarly account.

• Read Setting Up and Using Folio to learn about Folio.

• Complete a Word document with your photo, bio, and personal or professional mission statement. Upload the selected document to Folio.

• Complete the Starter Kit and Learning Strategies and Tools quiz.

On Thursday—Day 3

• Post your initial post in the Opportunities for Personal Development discussion.

• Read Chapter 3: Enjoying the College Experience.

• Read one of the three personal growth articles.

By Monday—Day 7

• Complete the Student Support Center learning activity.

• Complete the Student Support Network Plan journal.

• Read The Importance of a Social Support Network in Achieving Your Goals.

• Watch Social Well Being—Importance of Social Connections.

• Review the Support Network Scenarios handout.

On Monday—Day 7

• Post two responses to peers in the Opportunities for Personal Development discussion.

items.title: Week 2 - Starter Kit
items.plainText:
Week 2 - Starter Kit

Starter Kit—Week 2

• Introduction

• Starter Kit Activities

• Next Steps

Introduction

Welcome to your second week of GEN101! In Week 1 you got the “lay of the land”—in other words, you checked out your environment as you would at the beginning of any project or journey. You were introduced to the people involved—your peers and your instructor. You became familiar with your environment, the online classroom, and you did some self-exploration to help you define your “why.” You also learned some time management tips that you’ll be able to use throughout this course and in future courses.

By now, you should have set up your OFFICE 365 email account and registered for Live Learning. Remember, you are required to attend one Live Learning session within the five weeks of this course. If you can’t attend, there will be a recording available in Week 4 you can watch. Attendance is important because your participation in the Live Learning session is a part of your Personal Development Plan.

Before we begin Week 2, there are a few learning tools you will need to know to complete your assignments. You must complete these activities in your Week 2 Starter Kit before you begin your coursework. You will earn five points toward your final grade after completing them.

Meet a fellow student and join our community of learning.

Select the "Starter Kit Activities" tab at the top to continue.

Starter Kit Activities

Use the Firefox or Chrome browser to view the learning activity.

Select the "Next Steps" tab the top to continue.

Next Steps

Now that you have started your journey toward your college degree, it is important for you to know that you are not alone! UAGC provides support services and resources to help you accomplish your academic goals and enhance your personal growth. The Student Support Center (available from the link in the menu on the left) is the one-stop shop where you will find a variety of resources including, but not limited to, academic resources, career services resources, school-life balance tips, and how to contact UAGC tech support. You will have a chance to explore the Student Support Center this week. Additionally, you will reflect on the importance of achieving personal growth and development after reading stories of how some people enriched their lives through personal growth and development.

Finally, we want you to know that every course you take at UAGC will have a similar format. Everything you need to be successful in the course will be provided for you. You will find easy links to connect you with the course content, and the learning activities will have step-by-step instructions and rubrics to help you know how they will be graded.

Discussion Topic: Week 2 - Discussion ForumWeek 2 - Discussion Forum

Your initial discussion thread is due on Day 3 (Thursday) and you have until Day 7 (Monday) to respond to your classmates. Your grade will reflect both the quality of your initial post and the depth of your responses. Refer to the Discussion Forum Grading Rubric under the Settings icon for guidance on how your discussion will be evaluated.

This discussion will be graded on a 10-point rubric and is worth 5% of your course grade.

Opportunities for Personal Development [WLOs: 2, 4] [CLO: 2]

| Prepare

Prior to working on this discussion,

• Read Chapter 3 in your textbook: Enjoying the College Experience.

• Choose one of the following personal growth articles that interests you. You will use this article for your initial post.

• Personal Growth: A Definite Guide to Self Growth

• What Is Personal Growth and Why Is It So Important?

• The Importance of Self-Improvement and Personal Growth

• Review the rubric used to grade the discussion.

| Reflect

Enrolling in college ensures you will experience personal growth. In fact, seeking intellectual, emotional, and collaborative growth is likely the reason you decided to become a student. Personal growth during your higher education experience will enrich your personal and professional life. It is your personal experiences that help you become better individuals, as members of families, communities, and society. As you read your selected the personal growth article, consider the importance of your growth and how, through your learning and actions, you can reach your fullest potential.

| Write

In your initial discussion forum post (of at least 250 words):

• Provide the title of the article you selected to read.

• Reflect on your chosen article and Chapter 3 of the textbook. In your reflection,

• Briefly describe what your selected article is about.

• Share two ideas from the article that impacted your thoughts about growth.

• Describe a growth experience you had recently or in the past.

• Discuss the personal and professional (career-related) growth you hope to achieve during your college journey.

• Explain how you plan to use your growth to enrich your family, community, and society.

| Guided Response

Respond substantively (in at least 100 words) to at least two of your peers by Day 7 (Monday).

Select two classmates who did not read the same article as you.

In your peer responses,

• Discuss something from your peers’ posts that resonates with you. Your peer may provide a new or different perspective, or explains something you can apply to your experience moving forward.

• Discuss how learning about others’ journeys, challenges, and growth contributes to your academic success in the online environment.

• Use Section 3.2 of Chapter 3: Enjoying the College Experience to support your discussion.

An important part of your learning involves the exchange of ideas and feedback with your peers and instructor. It is also tied to the participation and critical thinking components of your earned discussion grade.

Jan 19 9:36am | Last reply Jan 20 10:19pm
Manage Discussion by Kim King

Reply from Kim King

Article Title:The College Experience: Social Interaction, Diversity, and Growth

The article I selected focuses on the broader college experience and emphasizes that personal growth extends far beyond grades and coursework. It highlights how social interaction, relationship building, diversity, and inclusion play a significant role in shaping students both personally and professionally. The article explains that whether college is experienced on campus or online, meaningful engagement with peers and instructors are essential to reducing isolation, managing stress, and creating a supportive learning environment. This Chapter (3) of the textbook reinforces ideas by emphasizing self-awareness, communication, collaboration, and intentional engagement as key parts of personal growth during the college experience.

Two ideas from the article strongly impacted my understanding of growth. First, the idea that social interaction is not optional but essential which stood out to me, my Bishop often says that communication is key. The article explains that building relationships through discussions, study groups, and collaborative learning not only improves academic success but also reduces stress and increases confidence. Second, the emphasis on diversity and inclusion reshaped how I view growth. Learning alongside people from different backgrounds, perspectives, and life experiences have expanded critical thinking and encourages my openness, empathy, and adaptability, all of which are necessary for both personal development and professional success.

A meaningful growth experience I have had was returning to college after the tragic loss of my son the last I attended college which was also started in January, family, church involvement, and personal healing. Navigating these responsibilities have required much discipline, emotional regulation, and stronger communication skills I wasn't sure I was ready to embrace , particularly in managing stress somewhat and remaining present instead of allowing that past trauma to interfere with my current goals. This experience taught me the importance of asking for support and engaging with others rather than trying to handle everything independently like I was use too and still struggle with sometimes.

During my college journey, I hope to continue growing personally by strengthening my confidence, emotional resilience, and self-awareness. Professionally, I aim to enhance my leadership, communication, and collaboration skills so I can be effective in service-oriented and community-focused work. I also hope to develop a deeper appreciation for diversity and inclusive practices that prepare me to work with people from all walks of life.

I plan to use this growth to enrich my family by modeling perseverance, adaptability, and lifelong learning. Within my community, I intend to apply what I learn to support, advocate for, and uplift others through informed service and collaboration. Ultimately, my growth will allow me to contribute positively to society by promoting inclusion, empathy, and meaningful connection in both my personal and professional roles.

The title of the article I chose to read is “Personal Growth: A Definite Guide to Self Growth.” This article explains the true meaning of personal growth and goes into detail about the process and the positive changes it can create in a person’s life. It helped me better understand that personal growth is not something that happens overnight, but rather something that takes time, effort, and self-awareness.

One of the most impactful ideas shared in the article was “turning dead time into a time of self-improvement.” This idea really stood out to me because it made me reflect on how much time we waste throughout the day without realizing it. Instead of using that time scrolling on my phone or doing unproductive things, I could be using it to work toward my goals. This motivated me to want to be more intentional with my daily routine and focus on activities that will benefit me in the long run.

Another important point discussed was identifying personal strengths and weaknesses. During a recent class activity, we did something similar, and I realized how helpful it was. It allowed me to recognize areas where I needed improvement, but also appreciate the strengths I already have. Since then, I’ve noticed improvement both at work and in my personal life.

Moving forward, I plan to become more structured with my nail business and work toward growing it consistently. I also hope to advance within my current job and eventually move into a leadership role. Lastly, I plan to share what I’ve learned with friends and family by encouraging them to become more self-aware and intentional about their own personal growth, while continuing to work on myself as well.

Kylie Fugate (she/her/hers)

Jan 16 4:10pm | Last reply Jan 17 12:04pm
Manage Discussion by Kylie Fugate

Reply from Kylie Fugate

The article I selected is What Is Personal Growth and Why Is It So Important? In this Mindvalley Blog article, Amy White discusses the definition of personal growth, the types which include improving social skills and boosting your overall health and well-being, and examples of goals that one can set to improve their personal development. White also includes resources to help with personal development and ways to make a plan.

The two ideas that While expressed that caught my eye were the explanation of why self-growth is important and the method of going out of your comfort zone. While everyone knows the basics of personal growth, understanding why it’s important and why it should be done is crucial to implementing it into one’s life properly. Understanding the importance of personal growth will allow for healthier relationships, career success, financial success, and a healthier life in general. By going out of one’s comfort zone, they are able to challenge themselves. This may look like saying hi to someone new, enjoying a new meal, paying someone a compliment, or taking up a new course to improve skills. Slowly building new habits will end up creating new versions of ourselves.

Within the last six months, I have had some major life changes including personal growth. After living in the same town since the day I was brought home from the hospital, I moved away to another state. Doing this led to a realization of how poor of a mental state I had been in and I sought out professional help with the support of those around me. I began therapy as well, which has been helpful. I also re-enrolled in college to pursue an area of studies that has been heavy on my heart since early high school: criminal justice. I am nowhere near completing the goals I have set for myself, but I am on the right path.

During this academic journey, I am hoping to maintain and earn good grades and persevere through any challenges that may arise. I am also hoping to begin training for the police academy so that I can get a jumpstart on entering the criminal justice field. I also have the goal to apply what is learned from my education to my personal life - even if it’s miniscule things. For example, learning about socialization this week has led to me joining a book club which has increased my socialization tremendously.

By earning a degree and improving myself, I will be able to better provide for my family. I also will be able to better my community through helping protect it as a police officer and detective. I also want to change the viewpoints of society on criminal behavior as it is often connected to mental illness and lack of support. In my personal life, I hope to gain a better mental state - although I am on the right path - and maintain healthy relationships.

Lizeth Elizondo

Jan 15 11:13pm | Last reply Jan 19 8:03pm
Manage Discussion by Lizeth Elizondo

Reply from Lizeth Elizondo

After reading the article "Personal Growth: A Definite Guide To Self Growth" and thinking about my own experiences, I realized one thing. Personal growth never really ends.

The article points out that successful people strive for personal growth in their lives and careers, but it is not a one-size-fits-all approach. It offers a clear guide to starting your personal growth journey and reminds me of the SMART technique we learned earlier.

In the article, Furqan Ashiq talks about observing other people's lives. It reminded me of the advice one of my mentors gave me when I had my first job. At the time, benchmarking was all the rage in the production industry. He suggested I implement this in my personal life: learn from other people’s mistakes and put myself in their shoes as if I had made the mistake. Is that something that I wanted in my life? Could I avoid hardships by observing others? It is very difficult, since you have to set your judgments aside and look at the situation without bias.

At the time, I became more proactive at work and began thinking about how to stay one step ahead of my supervisor. I started showing up to work consistently on time, worked on my communication skills, and wrote emails to my colleagues and supervisor more frequently.

I also started therapy to work on issues that were holding me back. I became financially literate and began paying off my debt. I knew I needed a better-paying job to get out of debt faster, and while still at my current position, I continued polishing those skills.

This helped me when I got married and started a family. Still, as the article points out, personal, physical, and social growth is ongoing.

What I did not see mentioned in any of the articles is that personal growth is not linear and sometimes lonely and messy. As a new mom, all the skills I had learned before did not help me at all. I developed postpartum mood disorder, and all the therapy I had done did not prepare me for that stage in my life. I had to start again, develop new tools, and learn to navigate this new chapter of my life.

My main goal is maximize time with my family and reduce work hours. To do this, I need to increase my income. Earning a Bachelor’s in Instructional Design would help me to create coursework that generates passive income, mentor new interpreters, and support people with limited English proficiency in breaking down communication barriers.

I also want to show my children that learning is an ongoing journey and to instill curiosity and a love of learning by setting a good example.

I really want them to see me graduate.

The article that stood out to me was What Is Personal Growth and Why Is It So Important? It explains personal growth as an ongoing process of learning more about yourself and working toward becoming a better version of who you are. It talks about how personal growth involves developing positive habits, improving your mindset, and learning from both successes and failures. The article also stresses that growth doesn’t happen instantly it takes time, patience, and effort.

Two ideas from the article really stood out to me. One was the importance of self-awareness. The article explained that understanding your strengths, weaknesses, and values is key to personal growth. This made me realize that growth starts with being honest with yourself, even when it’s uncomfortable. Another idea that impacted me was the reminder that growth often comes from stepping outside of your comfort zone. Instead of avoiding challenges, the article encourages embracing them because they help you learn and build confidence.

One growth experience I had was adjusting to increased responsibility in my life. There was a time when I had to balance school, work, and family responsibilities all at once, and it felt overwhelming. Over time, I learned how to manage my time better, stay organized, and ask for help when I needed it. That experience helped me grow into someone who is more responsible and better at handling stress.

During my college journey, I hope to grow personally by becoming more confident, improving my communication skills, and staying motivated even when things get difficult. Professionally, I want to gain knowledge and skills that will prepare me for my future career and help me become dependable and goal-oriented. I plan to use my growth to support my family, give back to my community, and contribute positively to society by being someone who is reliable, compassionate, and willing to help others when I can.

The article that stuck out the most to me was the MindValley Blog. This article is mainly about personal growth, and whether your transformation is positive, mental, physical, or spiritual. Two ideas that impacted my thoughts about growth were finding out my personal growth level and writing down my life growth goals. This is important to me because it makes me have to work harder for what I truly want but the personal growth level helps me find out where to start. A growth experience I had in the past was learning how to manage and maintain being a single parent due to an unexpected loss. My children’s father passed away unexpectedly a few years ago, and during that time, I was a homemaker (stay at home mom), and I didn’t even know where to start. I had to get an overnight job at McDonald’s, and went to school during the day. I took an accelerated MA program so I can have a career that could provide for myself and children. Those who knew me in my past can really see my growth over the last 6 years, and I am proud of how far I have come. During my college journey, I hope to achieve to be more open to talking to my peers, and grow those lasting relationships. I also want to achieve by graduating in a few years, and then going back to school again, ultimately for a Masters degree in Nutrition and Dietetics. I want to help my community by encouraging healthier eating habits, and providing information to them to nourish their bodies, and encourage foods and nutrients as Nature’s natural medications.

Reply from Niki Woodward

The article “The Importance of Self-Improvement and Personal Growth” focuses on the idea that personal growth should be something that you do all the time and it should be intentional, not something that stops when you’re finished with school or training. It highlights that successful people invest in themselves by having better habits, self-reflection, and learning new skills. One idea that stood out to me was that personal growth starts from the inside but will eventually impact every area of life. Another takeaway is the idea that growth requires getting out of one’s comfort zone and self-reflection in order to reach your full potential.

Chapter 3 of the textbook, “Enjoying the College Experience”, discusses the importance of learning from others and viewing things from other people’s perspectives, especially in an online environment. This is something that I can connect with. In my professional life, I try to view situations from other people’s perspectives because I would want the same respect in return. That was not always the case when I was younger. I used to care more about being right than being fair, without realizing how closed-minded that mindset could be or how it could hurt others. Growth often comes from moments that force you to reflect, and I’ve learned that empathy and perspective lead to better outcomes both personally and professionally.

A recent growth experience for me was failing my Body Composition Assessment (BCA) after returning home from deployment. I was devastated, even though I knew deep down it was a possibility. That experience opened my eyes to how my choices could negatively impact not only my career but also my long-term health. Instead of letting it define me, I made changes to my diet and got back into the gym. I lost 44 pounds, returned to weight standards, and scored an outstanding high on my physical fitness test. This had a positive effect on me by increasing my self-confidence and it motivated me to help others by teaching ShipShape classes to Sailors to help improve their health.

Professionally, one area of growth that I want to achieve during my college journey is figuring out what I want to be “when I grow up”. I am currently looking into Emergency Management, Human Resources, and Healthcare Administration. My goal is to gain some sense of clarity during my undergraduate program. I plan to use this growth to enrich my family by being a role model to our children, showing that you can go back to school when your “old” (as they like to call me) and change your career path-it’s never to late. I also hope to support my community through my leadership and mentorship and contribute to society by leading with empathy, holding myself accountable and helping others grow through my own life experiences.

Jan 15 3:46pm | Last reply Jan 15 8:34pm
Manage Discussion by Sharmayne Morgan

Reply from Sharmayne Morgan

Article Title: What Is Personal Growth & Why Is It So Important?

The article I selected explores the deeper meaning of personal growth and its significance in living a meaningful life. It explains that growth isn’t just about improving one skill or fixing a weakness; it’s about understanding ourselves more honestly and choosing to evolve in ways that support our well‑being, relationships, and purpose. The article also emphasizes that growth touches every part of who we are: mentally, physically, emotionally, and socially.

Two ideas from the article really stayed with me. The first is that personal growth is a lifelong journey. There’s no point where we “finish” becoming who we’re meant to be. Instead, we continue to learn, adjust, and become stronger through every experience. The second idea is that real growth often requires stepping into discomfort. That resonated with me because the moments that shaped me the most weren’t easy, but they pushed me to become better.

When I think about my own growth, two experiences stand out. One is becoming a better leader for my Marines. I realized leadership goes beyond giving orders; it means showing up consistently, listening to their concerns, understanding their goals, and guiding them so each person can succeed. For example, after noticing a team member struggling, I made time to talk privately, listened, and helped them with solutions, which built greater trust. Actively listening has helped me build trust and support those who rely on me. The second experience is choosing to prioritize my physical and mental health after a difficult year. Making the decision to take care of myself was a turning point, and it’s something I’m doing not just for today, but for my future self.

As I move through my college journey, I hope to continue growing both personally and professionally. I want to build knowledge that strengthens my leadership, deepens my empathy, and helps me become someone who can make a positive difference in the lives of others. My goal is to use what I learn to support my family, uplift my community, and contribute to society in a meaningful way.

For me, growth is about becoming someone who not only improves their own life but also helps others rise with them. That’s the kind of person I’m working toward becoming because I believe real progress is measured by the positive impact we have on those around us.

Patrick Camacho

Jan 15 12:28pm | Last reply Jan 19 12:44pm
Manage Discussion by Patrick Camacho

Reply from Patrick Camacho

Title of the Article Selected

Personal Growth: A Definite Guide to Self-Growth (Mindvalley)

Reflection on the Article

The article "Personal Growth: A Definite Guide to Self-Growth" defines personal growth as a lifelong, intentional process focused on self-awareness, discipline, and continuous improvement. Rather than viewing growth as something that happens naturally, the article emphasizes that meaningful change comes from deliberate daily actions, reflection, and accountability. It highlights how personal growth influences multiple areas of life, including health, relationships, mindset, and professional success.

Key Ideas That Impacted My View on Growth

The balance between discipline and motivation resonated with me. One's motivations for self-growth may change, but self-discipline will enable you to persevere when your motivation and enthusiasm wane. The idea that growth often requires discomfort is very accurate. We, humans, are creatures of comfort. In most cases, we resist change, and when things are going well, we are even more resistant to it (even if we are in a bad situation). For many of us, it is only through pain and discomfort that we ultimately develop the self-discipline and motivation necessary for personal growth.

One key idea from the article that impacted me is the emphasis on discipline over motivation. Discipline is how it allows power to continue even when motivations fluctuate and enthusiasm fades. Another essential idea is that growth often requires discomfort. The article explains that stepping outside of comfort zones is necessary for lasting improvement and self-development.

A Personal Growth Experience

I have had many personal growth experiences, but one of the most recent and life-changing has been my weight-loss journey. Weight control has always been a challenge; losing 10-20 pounds, only to gain 30, results in an endless cycle that often leads to regaining more than what was lost. The push I needed came when I realized I had gained 30 pounds in a couple of months (yes, stress and other factors contributed to the weight gain). By the end of May, I'd had enough; from June 2024 to February 2025, I lost 102 pounds, reducing my weight from 282 to 180. With the help of my PPC, I initially began with the support of a weight-loss medication and walking more. Yes, simply walking, but walking with a purpose. 20 20-minute miles turned into 17-minute miles, then 15-minute miles, which turned into 13.5 minutes. From June to December, I dropped 60 pounds, and my daily routine consisted of regular exercise, calorie tracking, and lots of strict self-discipline, and I tapered off the medication. This experience showed me that sustainable change comes from consistent habits rather than short-term solutions.

Personal and Professional Growth Goals During College

My personal growth goals during college include continuing to develop my critical thinking, leadership, and discipline so I have the fortitude to push through when enthusiasm fades.

Professionally, I want to strengthen my academic foundation to support long-term career growth and entrepreneurial goals. I aim to improve my time management, resilience, and adaptability so I can balance responsibilities effectively while continuing to grow.

Using Growth to Enrich Family, Community, and Society

I plan to leverage my personal and professional growth to make a positive impact by continuing to develop my non-profit foundation. The foundation's mission is to protect and empower those who serve—both human and canine—through structured K9 rescue efforts, support for veteran-owned companies, K9 training initiatives, and community outreach. As the foundation grows to fill the picture I have in my mind for it, my continued personal growth will continue to define the foundation's long-term vision of service, leadership, and community impact. Through this work, I aim to enrich my family, strengthen communities, and make a meaningful contribution to society.

Vantino Begaye

Jan 14 10:26am
Manage Discussion by Vantino Begaye

Reply from Vantino Begaye

SELF-IMPROVEMENT AND PERSONAL GROWTH

The pursuit of “Self-improvement” and “Personal Growth” is not a desirable hobby or a some trend, it stands as the most fundamental, commitment an individual can make to ensure a rich, resilient, and deeply satisfying existence. This intentional journey forms the essential framework for genuine human, acting as the catalyst that unlocks our full potential across every domain of mental, professional, relational, and spiritual. By consistently and actively engaging in this process, we cease to be passive people in our own lives and instead become the conscious build of our future selves. This commitment, while demanding, is the clearest pathway to transcending limits and achieving a true purpose in life.

The benefits derived from this continuous investment are multifaceted and deeply interconnected. Firstly, self-improvement is inextricably linked to enhanced mental well-being The practice of growth often begins with cultivating self-awareness a process of understanding our core values, emotional triggers, cognitive stress and inherent strengths. This introspective work is crucial because it allows us to manage internal conflict and reduce unnecessary stress. Techniques often incorporated into this domain, such as mindfulness, meditation, and keeping a journal are powerful tools for developing emotional regulation. When we understand why we react the way we do, we can choose a more constructive response, moving from reactivity to thoughtful action. This reduction in internal chaos creates a stable mental environment, which is the prerequisite for all other forms of success. Furthermore, actively learning and challenging the brain, such as acquiring a new language or mastering a complex skill, fosters neuroplasticit keeping the mind sharp, agile, and less sus to the cognitive decline associated with stagnation. The psychological reward of meeting a personal challenge reinforces self-efficacy and builds genuine, lasting confidence, which is far more stable than confidence based solely on external validation.

Secondly, the commitment to growth is the engine of professional success. In the rapidly evolving modern economy, standing still is functionally moving backward. Self-improvement, in a professional context, means actively embracing continuous learninga practice

Personal Growth: A Definite Guide to Self Growth

The article discusses the crucial role of self-growth, a lifelong journey of spiritual, physical, and social development in achieving daily happiness and reaching one's full potential. To begin, it outlines a practical approach starting with self-reflection questions designed to help individuals identify areas they wish to improve. The author also provides practical tips, such as observing others for self-awareness and turning unproductive 'dead time' into opportunities for self-empowerment by listening to educational content.

The article's point about observing others to "understand what needs to be improved within you" deeply resonated with me. This principle is just as relevant at home, where I can replace time wasted on social media with activities that will help me work on becoming the best version of myself.

A recent and significant growth experience for me has been learning to be okay with things not getting done in order to prioritize myself. I used to feel constant pressure to be productive, but I've learned that allowing myself to rest and recharge is just as important.

My personal growth goal is to continue focusing on my well-being, while my professional growth goal is to improve my time management and prioritization. Focusing on my own well-being helps me be a more positive person for my family. This personal growth then allows me to inspire my community, society and contribute in a more meaningful way.

items.plainText:
Week 2 - Learning Activity

• Due Jan 19 at 11:59pm

• Points3

• Questions3

• Time Limit None

• Allowed Attempts Unlimited

Instructions

Student Support Center

[WLOs: 3, 4] [CLO: 2]

The famous poet John Donne said, “No man is an Island.” You may think you’re on an island as an online learner, but you really are not! UAGC provides a wealth of support services and resources to help you accomplish your academic goals and enhance your personal growth.

In this learning activity, you will familiarize yourself with the Student Support Center which is a one-stop shop with many resources, including academic and career-service resources, school-life balance tips, and contact information for UAGC tech support. The Student Support Center will be on the menu bar of each of your classes. The Student Support Center (SSC) will also help prepare you as you design your Personal Development Plan in Week 5, in addition to future professional success.

To access the Student Support Center, select the “Student Support Center” link on the left in your classroom.

Prepare

• Visit the Student Support Center (SSC) and explore the different tools and resources the SSC has to offer.

• Watch the Student Support Center video and visit the Student Support Center to explore the many different tools and resources the SSC has to offer.

• Reflect on the resources in the “Supports & Resources,” “Academic & Career Skills,” and “School-Life Balance” sections of the SSC.

• Identify at least three tools or resources that you find particularly valuable and useful. The quiz will ask you to list these.

After participating in this learning activity, move on to complete the following quiz by Day 7. You will have unlimited time and unlimited attempts.

The quiz contains three questions about resources you explored in the SSC and is worth 5% of your grade. On each attempt you will only answer the questions you have not answered or that you answered incorrectly. To begin the quiz, select Take the Quiz. When you are finished, select Submit Quiz.

items.title: Week 2 - Journal
items.plainText:
Student Support Network Plan

[WLO: 1] [CLO: 3]

“

Life can only be understood backwards; but it must be lived forwards.

”

— Soren Kierkegaard

This week, you have an opportunity to practice reflective thinking. Reflective thinking is an effective strategy to enhance your personal growth and development. When things go well, reflection helps you identify behaviors or practices you can repeat to accomplish the same or similar results in the future. When things do not go well, reflection helps you identify the source of the problem. Then you can consider how to achieve a better result in the future.

For this journal, you will develop a plan because creating a student support network is vital to your success at the University of Arizona Global Campus. Your support network will include the UAGC Student Support Center and others who can provide technical, emotional, and assignment support.

| Prepare

Prior to working on this journal, complete the following steps:

• Complete the Student Support Center Interactive learning activity.

• The Student Support Center can be accessed through the left navigation menu.

• Read The Importance of a Social Support Network in Achieving Your Goals.

• Watch Social well being - Importance of social connections - Social life - Social interactions.

• Review the .

• Think about how the people described in the Support Network Scenarios sheet could use their support network, instructor, or the UAGC resources for help.

| Reflect

Think about a time in your life when a support network was critical to your success. You will share only what you are comfortable sharing in this assignment.

Creating your own student support network is vital to your success at the University of Arizona Global Campus. Your network should include people who can give you technical support, emotional support, or assignment support.

| Write

To complete your journal,

• Download the template.

• Respond to each section.

• Save the completed template to your computer and upload it to Waypoint. Be sure to double-check that it was properly saved and successfully uploaded.

In the Student Support Network Plan template, you will:

• Identify an event, situation, or experience in your life where your support network played a critical role in your success.

• Explain two or three specific ways your support network played a critical role in your success.

• Select one scenario from the . Identify the scenario you have selected and identify two to three strategies a student could use to overcome the stressful situation in the scenario.

• Discuss one way a support network, like an instructor, UAGC student resource, or accountability partner, could help you on your academic journey to earn a degree. This may include resources that could assist you with finding time to study, read, and write.

• Discuss why having a plan is important for handling stressful situations.

• Explain how your social support network can help you during a stressful or challenging time.

• List the social support networks you belong to.

Journal Requirements

• Research and Resource Expectations:

• Sources are not required for your journals.

• Paper Expectations:

• Assignment must be completed using the template.

• Remember to delete the template prompts before submitting your assignment.

Next Steps

• Refer to the Journal Grading Rubric for guidance on how your journal will be evaluated.

• Submit your journal (using the template) to Waypoint for evaluation no later than Day 7.

Need Help?

For more detailed instructions, select the following links:

• Waypoint Introduction to Waypoint and Originality Reports

• Grammarly

• UAGC Writing Center

• Submitting a paper to the Writing Center

• 24/7 Writing Tutoring

• Paper Review service

Waypoint Assignment Submission

The assignments in this course will be submitted to Waypoint. Please refer to the instructions below to submit your assignment.

• Click on the Assignment Submission button below. The Waypoint "Student Dashboard" will open in a new browser window.

• Browse for your assignment.

• Click Upload.

• Confirm that your assignment was successfully submitted by viewing the appropriate week's assignment tab in Waypoint.

For more detailed instructions, refer to the Waypoint Tutorial.

items.title: Live Learning Session
items.plainText:
Live Learning Session

Welcome to the Live Learning information page!

We are so excited that you will be joining us for synchronous sessions. This is a great opportunity to connect with faculty and other students to build collegiality and engagement that will be useful in all aspects of your life going forward.

Live Learning Sessions

As part of the learning experience, students in GEN 101 are required to take part in one Live Learning Session. These sessions give you a chance to connect with faculty and other students. You will also explore the connections between course content and your academic, professional, and personal life which will help you complete your Week 5 Personal Development Plan assignment and activities.

How do I access the sessions?

Click on the Live Learning link in the left navigation bar of the course for details. Sessions are held via Zoom. You will need your @student.uagc.edu email address to log in to the Live Learning session.

We look forward to seeing you in a Live Learning Session!

Please click here for a guide around accessing the Live Learning sessions in Zoom.

***IMPORTANT***

Please note you must use your @student.uagc.edu email address to register and to access live learning sessions. If you have not yet set up or used your @student.uagc.edu Zoom account, you will need to do so before you can attend these sessions. Click here to set up your Zoom account, and make sure to sign up using your @student.uagc.edu email address.

Weekly Learning Outcomes

This week you will

• Explain the importance of goal setting.

• Create an academic and professional SMART goal.

• Examine how reading and writing well is relevant academically and professionally.

• Assess personal strengths and opportunities for growth related to SMART goals.

Introduction

Welcome to the third week of your first course! Your academic journey continues with fellow classmates and your instructor as you begin to work on goal setting. Remember, engaging with others not only adds depth to your learning but also creates a sense of community and support with those who are also venturing into their college experience. To get you started there is a starter kit that will introduce you to goal setting.

This week's discussion will focus on “Looking Ahead,” not only in this course, but also in your career and your life. You will spend some time thinking about where you want to go and who can help get you there. You will also use what you learned in your starter kit and dive into goal setting this week. These goals are a big part of your Week 5 final project—the Personal Development Plan. What’s exciting about this plan is that it is something you can use long after you successfully complete this course. It’s time to get started. Open your mind, prepare yourself, and jump in with both feet!

|

Need Help?

Long description

The Student Advocate HELPline works with students during times of extreme need in order to address concerns for immediate safety, identify community resources, and address educational impacts of personal hardships.

• Suicidal Thoughts

• Grief/Loss

• Domestic Violence

• Homelessness

If you are struggling with one of the situations above or another hardship, submit an Online Care Report to connect with a Student Advocate.

Online Care Report Link

Need Help?

Long description

The Office of Student Access and Wellness works to support students with disabilities and chronic medical conditions to provide:

• Extended Time

• Accessible Materials

• Advocacy

items.title: Week 3 - Checklist
items.plainText:
Week 3 - Checklist

Week 3 Checklist

Effective time management is essential to student success. Balancing school, work, and personal responsibilities can be challenging but creating a personalized study plan at the beginning of each week is helpful. Each week, your instructor will provide a checklist of assignments and learning activities for the week, and their due dates. Here is the checklist for Week 3:

By Wednesday—Day 2

Read the Overview and watch the video.

• Complete the SMART Goals Starter Kit learning activity.

• Watch the video, SMART Goals.

• Complete the Professional SMART Goals interactive.

• Complete the Academic SMART Goals interactive.

• Complete the Starter Kit SMART Goals quiz.

On Thursday—Day 3

• Post your initial post in the Looking Ahead discussion forum

• Read sections 1.2 and 1.3 of Chapter 1: Developing a Plan for Success.

• Read section 13.1 of Chapter 13: Career Planning and Professional Development.

• Review the Final Project.

By Thursday—Day 3

• Complete the Writing Center learning activity.

By Monday—Day 7

• Complete the Creating Your Smart Goals assignment.

• Review sections 1.2 and 1.3 of Chapter 1: Developing a Plan for Success.

• Read How to write SMART goals in 5 steps (with examples).

• Watch the SMART Goals video.

• Complete the Reading Strategies quiz.

• Watch SQ3R Reading Method.

• Read Chapter 8: Reading Strategies.

• Post two responses to peers in the Looking Ahead Discussion forum.

items.title: Week 3 - Starter Kit
items.plainText:
Week 3 - Starter Kit

Starter Kit—Week 3

• Introduction

• Professional SMART Goals

• Academic SMART Goals

Introduction

Welcome to your third week of GEN101. Congratulations, you have accomplished much in Weeks 1 and 2. Your self-exploration with your SWOT Analysis and your attendance at a Live Learning session have prepared you for writing both an Academic and Professional personal SMART goal. If you have not attended a Live Learning session, please try to do so soon as it will help you complete this assignment. To begin that important process, the Week 3 Starter Kit gives you more information about not only the importance of writing goals, but also how to write goals.

You will hear how Dr. Newton Miller, one of our UAGC instructors, prepares his SMART goal, how Ed developed his Professional SMART goal with the help of his boss and how GEN developed her Academic Goal. Finally, at the end of the starter kit you will have an opportunity to review your draft of your professional and academic goals using the checklist at the end. So, don’t miss out on that opportunity to practice writing SMART goals!

| Prepare

Prior to working on this learning activity:

• Review Section 1.2: Setting Goals.

• Read How to write SMART goals in 5 steps (with examples).

• Complete "Professional SMART Goals".

• Complete "Academic SMART Goals".

Select the Professional SMART Goals tab at the top to continue.

To begin the Professional SMART Goals Interactive, click on the yellow "Start" button.

Use the Firefox or Chrome browser to view the learning activity.

Select the Academic SMART Goals tab at the top to continue.

To begin the Academic SMART Goals Interactive, click on the yellow "Start" button.

Use the Firefox or Chrome browser to view the learning activity.

Week 3 - Quiz 1 • Due Jan 21 at 11:59pm • Points2 • Questions3 • Time Limit None • Allowed Attempts Unlimited Instructions Starter Kit SMART Goals Quiz [WLOs: 1, 3] [CLOs: 1, 2] The SMART goal interactive located in the Week 3 Starter Kit was designed to help you learn to write professional and academic goals. At the end of each learning activity you were asked to practice writing a professional and academic goal. You will now upload the goals you wrote in this quiz to allow your instructor to provide feedback. This is an opportunity to get feedback on your SMART goals which will help you with your final assignment. You will complete a three-question short-answer quiz. This activity is worth 2% of your grade. | Prepare Prior to beginning the quiz, complete the Week 3 starter kit. | Write Complete your week 3 starter kit quiz by Day 2. You will upload your professional and academic SMART goals. The quiz contains three short-answer questions and is worth 2% of your course grade. You have unlimited time to complete the quiz once you begin; however, please be aware that Canvas may time out if left inactive for too long. It is recommended you draft your answers in a Word document before starting to ensure you have enough time to respond within the time limit. To receive instructor feedback on your goal, which can help you with your week three assignment, you must submit the quiz by the due date. Your quiz will be graded the following week. Tip: Write each goal in sentence form. Once you have done that, use the words from your sentence to check and ensure you have used all of the SMART components- specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound for each goal. Reminder: Your quiz submission will be in either a sentence or a couple of sentences for each goal. Follow your instructor's feedback when preparing your Week 3 assignment. Discussion Topic: Week 3 - Discussion ForumWeek 3 - Discussion Forum

Your initial discussion thread is due on Day 3 (Thursday) and you have until Day 7 (Monday) to respond to your classmates. Your grade will reflect both the quality of your initial post and the depth of your responses. Refer to the Discussion Forum Grading Rubric under the Settings icon for guidance on how your discussion will be evaluated.

This discussion will be graded on a 10-point rubric and is worth 6% of your course grade.

Looking Ahead [WLO: 4] [CLO: 3]

Where will you be in five years, ten years, or even twenty years? How do you even begin to think about the future you want for yourself? Consider working backward from your ideal future so that you can outline actions to take now to successfully reach your goal. SMART goals will help you articulate your path to success.

In this discussion you will review the professional/career goal you drafted for the SMART Goals Starter Kit learning activity. The work you are doing this week supports your Final Project which means that you are not only looking ahead but also thinking and working ahead!

Every day you set, pursue, and achieve goals. In fact, this course is evidence that you set a goal to pursue a college degree. Did you know there are short-term goals and long-term goals? For example, a short-term goal is to complete your work each day for this course. An example of a long-term goal is to complete your Final Project in Week 5. An even longer-term goal is to earn your college degree! Understanding the steps needed to achieve your long-term goals and short-term goals is important for your success.

Consider how you will dedicate time and effort to complete coursework. Do you have a study schedule? What will you do if an obstacle arises? How are your writing and reading comprehension skills? Will you need to schedule time to explore the resources provided in the Student Support Center or get help from the Paper Review service at the UAGC Writing Center? The Writing Center’s24/7 Writing Tutoring and Paper Review services can be accessed through the Writing Center & Library link in the left navigation menu.

Think about your professional and career plans. Consider how your college degree will help you achieve an initial career goal. For those of you further along on your career path, consider how your degree will help you achieve professional goals.

| Prepare

Prior to working on this discussion:

• Read sections 1.2 and 1.3 of Chapter 1: Developing a Plan for Success.

• Read section 13.1 of Chapter 13: Career Planning and Professional Development.

• Review the rubric used to grade the discussion.

| Write

In your post (250 words),

• Name the job title/occupation/career you want to have in five years. What is your long-term professional/career goal? If you are starting out, share your career goal. If your goal is to maintain your present job/career or if you are seeking an internal promotion you can share that goal.

• Use O*Net Online to find an occupational job listing that is similar to your anticipated or current job. Review the qualifications for the occupation/career you want in five years. Explain three skills that you will need to learn.

• Name a professional associations you could join or a business owner you could shadow to help you achieve your goals.

• Add your professional goal draft from the SMART Goals Starter Kit learning activity.

• Describe two people or professional resources who can help you achieve your professional goal.

• Reflect on what you have learned so far in this course. Use the template below to list three potential roadblocks you may encounter as you work toward the goal of completing your Final Project and three strategies you can use to overcome the roadblocks. Your strategies will give you a plan for success. You may highlight, copy, and paste the template into a separate document and attach it to your post, or you can paste the template directly into your discussion post.

Potential Roadblock

Strategy

TIP

You may want to take a screenshot of your final work or print it and hang it on a mirror you look at frequently as a reminder that you have a solution if roadblocks arise (and they will!).

| Guided Response

• Respond substantively (in at least 100 words) to at least two of your peers by Day 7 (Monday).

• In your peer responses,

• Review their professional goal and identify all of the components of a SMART goal (specific, measurable, achievable, relevant and time-bound). Make recommendations to your peer to ensure their goal is SMART.

• Recommend a national professional organization or conference they could attend to help them with their long-term career goal.

Christopher Sanders (he/him/his)

Jan 27 4:56pm
Manage Discussion by Christopher Sanders

Reply from Christopher Sanders

My Career Goal: In five years, I see myself working as a Cybersecurity Analyst or Penetration Tester. My long-term professional goal is to secure a stable position in an organization where I can actively monitor networks for security breaches and protect sensitive data from cyber threats. I am currently just starting out on this specific path, so my goal is to gain the necessary education and certifications to break into the industry by 2030.

I reviewed the O*NET Online listing for Penetration Testers. Three specific skills I will need to learn and strengthen are:

Transaction Security and Virus Protection Software: I need to become proficient with specific security tools listed in the industry requirements, such as Metasploit, Rapid7, or Tenable Nessus, to simulate attacks and identify vulnerabilities.

Scripting and Programming: The role requires knowledge of various development environments and operating system software. I need to strengthen my skills in Microsoft PowerShell, Bash, and languages such as Python or Ruby to write custom scripts for testing purposes.

Security Analysis: A core task is to analyze the security of systems, networks, and data. I need to develop the ability to conduct simulated cyberattacks and then analyze the risk to minimize potential losses or damages.

Professional Association: A professional association I could join to help me achieve my goals is the Information Systems Security Association (ISSA). Additionally, organizations like the EC-Council are relevant because they oversee the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH) credential I plan to earn.

My Professional SMART Goal: "I aim to secure an entry-level position as a Cybersecurity Analyst or a Junior Ethical Hacker by October 2030. To make myself a strong candidate, I will achieve two industry certifications: the CompTIA Security+ by the time I graduate, and the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH) within six months after graduation. Additionally, I will successfully complete my required Capstone project during my final year of the B.S. program."

Support System: Two people or resources that can help me achieve this include:

My Academic Advisor: They can help me plan my course schedule to ensure I finish my B.S. program on time and select electives that align with the Security+ certification.

A Career Mentor: By joining a local chapter of a professional association, I can find a mentor currently working in the field who can offer advice on navigating the job market and shadowing opportunities.

Roadblocks and Strategies: Reflecting on the course so far, specifically the chapter on overcoming roadblocks, here is my plan for success:

Balancing Certifications with Coursework: Studying for the Security+ exam while finishing my degree might become overwhelming. To overcome this, I will utilize time management and chunking strategies to break down the large goal of "studying" into small, daily 30-minute sessions dedicated specifically to certification preparation, separate from my degree homework.

Financial Constraints: Certification exams and study materials can be expensive. I plan to use resource utilization strategies by researching scholarship opportunities and utilizing free library resources or student discounts for exam vouchers.

Burnout and Loss of Motivation: The technical difficulty of cybersecurity can be discouraging. I will use growth mindset techniques to view difficult concepts as challenges rather than failures and rely on my peer support network to stay accountable.

Reply from Kylie Fugate

My career goals within the next five years are to enter the criminal justice career field and become a detective. Through this, I hope to help solve missing persons cases as well as cases that have run cold. Becoming a detective will require critical thinking, complex problem solving, and knowledge within areas such as psychology and law enforcement. I will have to improve my communication skills as well as build proper critical thinking and complex problem solving skills. Reply from Lizeth Elizondo

In five years, I see myself working as a Spanish staff interpreter in the Tucson Federal Court. I need to gain experience as a staff interpreter to fulfill my long-term goal of providing 40-hour medical interpretation training to bilingual personnel working in clinics and hospitals.

The position of staff Federal court interpreter requires several qualifications that I already possess. These include the ability to interpret simultaneously (at the same time the judge or counsel is speaking); consecutively (when a witness is giving testimony, I take notes and render the message in English to be entered into the record); to provide sight translations of forms and complex documents; and to translate documents written in a wide variety of registers when needed.

The only requirement I need is the Court Interpreter Certification issued by the Administrative Office of the United States Courts. This is a very rigorous process. I have already passed my written test, which allowed me to move on to the next step: the oral exam. I need to pass it with a minimum score of 80% to obtain my certification. This exam has a very low passing rate, similar to the Bar Exam.

I have passed my state exam and am now credentialed in the state of Arizona to interpret in court. Last year, I took the oral exam, knowing it would be very challenging. But I wanted to know where I stood. I have attended training courses and seminars, and I currently work as an independent contractor in immigration court, which provides the necessary experience I need to work at the Federal Court. I have also joined several study groups led by other colleagues. I will dedicate at least one hour a day to studying Spanish grammar and legal terminology, as well as practice taking mock tests to prepare for the exam this summer.

The exam dates have not been announced. The registration window runs from June 22 to July 31, 2026.

I have also joined the Arizona Translators and Interpreters and the National Association of Judicial Interpreters and Translators organizations to expand my network and learn more about the profession.

I also shadow Ignacio Barrientos, a federally certified interpreter and family friend, at the Federal Court when time allows. I will also take a one-on-one coaching session with a colleague who passed the exam with a 95% score to help me identify areas for improvement and better prepare for the exam. I also have a study partner who works in the Juvenile Court to review our practice test on Saturdays.

Professional SMART Goals:

• Specific: Study for at least one hour a day, Monday to Friday, until July 31, 2026.

• Measurable: Pass 10 mock exams with at least 85% consistently before August.

• Achievable: Listen to legal podcasts while cleaning, cooking, or driving. Read crime novels in Spanish to improve my vocabulary.

• Realistic: Since January 2025, I have been studying and preparing to take the test. Now that I have taken the test and know I scored 74% on my previous exam, I am confident I have what it takes to pass this year's exam.

• Time-bound: I have created a weekly goals spreadsheet to track my commitment to studying for at least one hour each day until August, 2026.

During these three weeks, I realized that I need to shift from being a pressure cooker to being a more proactive learner who prioritizes time management. I need a more structured routine to counter the unpredictability of my work and family life to achieve my goals.

Potential Roadblock

Strategy

Mental load

Create routines for the kids and prepare simple recipes and meal prep during the week.

Unexpected schedule changes in my Agenda

Have a backup plan for childcare. And allocate time before 7 am for studying.

Not enough time

Enroll the kids in extracurricular activities like jujitsu and have my husband take them so that I can have more time to study.

According to ONET, three important skills for this job are leadership and supervision, knowledge of healthcare regulations, and strong communication and organizational skills. These are things that I currently use in my position and continue to work on through my duties.

Although I do not currently belong to any professional associations, one that I could join to support my goals is the American College of Healthcare Executives (ACHE). The organization provides leadership development, networking, and resources that are aligned with my interest in healthcare administration.

My professional SMART goal is during the next 12 months, I will research career options in Emergency Management, Human Resources, and Healthcare Administration by talking with mentors and researching career requirements to determine the best professional path for me.

Two people that can help me achieve this are my husband and my Officer in Charge (OIC). My husband completed his bachelor’s degree in business administration and retired from the military after 21 years. He gives me insight into balancing my school, career, and life after active-duty service. My OIC has experience in Healthcare Administration and can provide mentorship and guidance related to career and leadership.

Potential Roadblock Strategy________________________________________________________________________________

Surgery and recovery Plan schoolwork in advance and communicate with instructors

Balancing work, school, and family Use check lists and planners, use my support network

Feeling stressed and overwhelmed Use UAGC SSC resources and break things up into smaller tasks

Having a support system and a plan will help me stay focused and take some of the stress off me while I work toward my goals.

Fue Yang

Jan 18 12:57pm | Last reply Jan 22 5:19pm
Manage Discussion by Fue Yang

Reply from Fue Yang

My long-term goal as a parole agent is to become an expert in client reintegration. I want to help develop programs that support people's re-entry into civilization. My need to help others and commitment to social justice are my main goals. I would like to think I am dedicated to making sure clients get the support they need to succeed. To achieve this, I plan to pursue a career in criminal justice and engage in training to stay informed about best practices. I will seek internships and volunteer for opportunities to gain experience in the field that I am pursuing. I aim to build a professional network and seek mentorship from experienced parole professionals. I want to advance in a supervisory role within the next 5 to 10 years. Achieving this goal will help me further my career.

Potential Roadblock

Strategy

High Caseloads

Implement time management techniques, such as the Pomodoro Technique, to structure your workday efficiently and reduce burnout.

Resistance from Clients

Focus on developing strong relationships through active listening and empathy, creating an environment where clients feel safe

Emotional Stress and Burnout

Prioritize self-care by engaging in activities that promote mental and physical health, such as regular exercise, hobbies, and mindfulness practices.

Christopher Capellan (he/him/his)

Jan 17 11:49pm | Last reply Jan 21 12:45pm
Manage Discussion by Christopher Capellan

Reply from Christopher Capellan

In five years, I aim to become a Cybersecurity Analyst, specializing in protecting organizational networks from cyber threats. My long-term career goal is to obtain a senior-level cybersecurity position where I can design, implement, and monitor advanced security protocols to prevent breaches and ensure compliance with industry standards.

Using O*Net Online, a Cybersecurity Analyst role requires knowledge of network security, information systems, and threat detection. Three key skills I need to develop include:

• Network Security Management – Understanding firewalls, intrusion detection systems, and network monitoring tools.

• Incident Response and Threat Analysis – Being able to identify vulnerabilities and respond to cyber incidents effectively.

• Ethical Hacking/Penetration Testing – Using ethical hacking methods to proactively find and address system weaknesses.

To support my career growth, I plan to join (ISC)², a professional association for cybersecurity professionals, and seek opportunities to shadow a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) at a mid-sized company to observe advanced cybersecurity operations firsthand.

My professional SMART goal is: “I will earn the CompTIA Security+ certification within the next 12 months by completing a 12-week study plan, taking weekly practice exams, and passing the certification exam by December 2026.”

Two key resources to help me achieve this goal include my academic advisor, who can guide me through training programs, and an IT mentor, who has experience in cybersecurity operations and can provide feedback and guidance on real-world applications.

Potential Roadblocks and Strategies:

Potential Roadblock

Strategy

Balancing work and study time

Create a structured weekly schedule with dedicated study hours

Difficulty understanding technical concepts

Use online tutorials, study groups, and mentor guidance

Limited hands-on experience

Participate in labs, internships, or virtual cybersecurity simulations

This course has taught me the importance of goal-setting, planning, and leveraging support networks, which I will continue to use as I pursue my cybersecurity career.

items.title: Week 3 - Assignment
items.plainText:
Creating Your SMART Goals

[WLOs: 1, 4] [CLOs: 1, 3, 4]

You have started developing pieces of your final project. This week you have worked on developing academic and professional SMART goals and reflecting on their value. SMART goals must be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time bound. This assignment will help you continue to think about what you want to achieve so you can create your Personal Development Plan in Week 5.

| Prepare

Prior to working on this assignment:

• Review sections 1.2 and 1.3 of Chapter 1: Developing a Plan for Success.

• Read How to write SMART goals in 5 steps (with examples).

• Complete the SMART Goals Starter Kit learning activity and quiz.

• Review the grading rubric for this assignment.

| Reflect

Think about what you want to accomplish as a college student. Earning credits and getting good grades is important, but so is the act of meaningful learning and application. You can set a professional SMART goal even if you’re not currently working in your dream profession. In fact, it’s helpful to set goals early in your career. If you’re not sure what you want to do professionally, consider crafting a goal related to skills, such as work ethic and time management.

| Write

• Download the and complete each section.

• Using the chart in the template, write out step-by-step components of your goal.

• Write a complete academic SMART goal in the template. Use your SMART goal from the Learning Activity Quiz and adjust with the feedback provided by your instructor.

• Complete the same process for your professional SMART goal.

• In a fully developed paragraph, discuss the value of setting SMART goals as you plan for academic and professional success.

• Save the completed template to your computer.

• Upload it to Waypoint.

• Be sure to double-check that it was properly saved and successfully uploaded.

Need Help?

Explore the links below for answers to the following questions:

• How do I save my template? View Downloading a Document

• I don’t have Microsoft Word; what do I do?

• Login to your Office 365 account using your UAGC student email address and student portal password.

• Select the “Install Office” link near the top. That will take you to the page where you can download the latest version of Office with the correct licensing.

• How do I turn in my work for grading? Watch Posting to a Discussion Forum.

• Where can I go for writing help? Visit The Writing Center.

• For more detailed instructions, refer to the Waypoint Tutorial.

Carefully review the Grading Rubric for the criteria that will be used to evaluate your assignment.

Waypoint Assignment Submission

The assignments in this course will be submitted to Waypoint. Please refer to the instructions below to submit your assignment.

• Click on the Assignment Submission button below. The Waypoint "Student Dashboard" will open in a new browser window.

• Browse for your assignment.

• Click Upload.

• Confirm that your assignment was successfully submitted by viewing the appropriate week's assignment tab in Waypoint.

For more detailed instructions, refer to the Waypoint Tutorial.

items.title: Live Learning Session
items.plainText:
Live Learning Session

Welcome to the Live Learning information page!

We are so excited that you will be joining us for synchronous sessions. This is a great opportunity to connect with faculty and other students to build collegiality and engagement that will be useful in all aspects of your life going forward.

Live Learning Sessions

As part of the learning experience, students in GEN 101 are required to take part in one Live Learning Session. These sessions give you a chance to connect with faculty and other students. You will also explore the connections between course content and your academic, professional, and personal life which will help you complete your Week 5 Personal Development Plan assignment and activities.

How do I access the sessions?

Click on the Live Learning link in the left navigation bar of the course for details. Sessions are held via Zoom. You will need your @student.uagc.edu email address to log in to the Live Learning session.

We look forward to seeing you in a Live Learning Session!

Please click here for a guide around accessing the Live Learning sessions in Zoom.

***IMPORTANT***

Please note you must use your @student.uagc.edu email address to register and to access live learning sessions. If you have not yet set up or used your @student.uagc.edu Zoom account, you will need to do so before you can attend these sessions. Click here to set up your Zoom account, and make sure to sign up using your @student.uagc.edu email address.

items.plainText:
Week 4 - Overview

Cultivating Growth

To be completed during the 4th week of class.

Overview

Assessment

Due Date

Format

Grading Percent

Academic Integrity

Day 2

Learning Activity

4

Growth Mindset

Day 3
(1st post)

Discussion Forum

Soft Skills

Day 3
(1st post)

Discussion Forum

4

Self-Assessment Analysis

Day 7

Assignment

9

Weekly Learning Outcomes

This week you will

• Discuss how soft skills promote academic and professional success.

• Identify the different behaviors of leaders and managers.

• Apply the principles of integrity to workplace and academic scenarios.

• Evaluate how mindset impacts achievement.

Introduction

Warning: This week will change your mind. In fact, it might even blow your mind. As Yoda from Star Wars says, “You must unlearn what you have learned.” How you think about yourself, your abilities, and your potential depends greatly on your mindset. This week, you will discover more about yourself and the impact your mindset has on your personal achievement. First, you will learn about integrity and why it matters. Then, you will discuss growth mindset and soft skills as tools you will need on your college journey. Go forth and conquer this week, and do as Yoda says in the video, Yoda & Growth Mindset, “Do, or do not. There is no try” (AES Middle School, 2014, 00:51).

This video may display YouTube ads.Continue to YouTube content

|

Need Help?

Long description

The Student Advocate HELPline works with students during times of extreme need in order to address concerns for immediate safety, identify community resources, and address educational impacts of personal hardships.

• Suicidal Thoughts

• Grief/Loss

• Domestic Violence

• Homelessness

If you are struggling with one of the situations above or another hardship, submit an Online Care Report to connect with a Student Advocate.

Online Care Report Link

Need Help?

Long description

The Office of Student Access and Wellness works to support students with disabilities and chronic medical conditions to provide:

• Extended Time

• Accessible Materials

• Advocacy

Accommodation form Link

items.title: Week 4 - Checklist
items.plainText:
Week 4 - Checklist

Week 4 Checklist

By Wednesday—Day 2

• Complete the Academic Integrity learning activity.

• Read Chapter 11: Acting with Integrity in your textbook.

• Review the Academic Integrity section of the UAGC catalog.

On Thursday—Day 3

• Complete the initial post for the Growth Mindset discussion forum.

• Read Chapter 10: Developing Resilience, Grit, and a Growth Mindset.

• Read Chapter 14: Support Networks, with a focus on Section 14.1.

• Read The Importance of a Social Support Network in Achieving Your Goals.

• Watch The Boost Students Need to Overcome Obstacles.

• Complete the first post for the Soft Skills discussion forum.

• Read Chapter 13: Career Planning and Professional Development, with a focus on Section 13.2 Professional Skill Building.

• Read Chapter 10: Developing Resilience, Grit, and A Growth Mindset, with a focus on Section 10.2 Growth Mindset.

• Read What are Soft Skills?.

• Watch Top 5 Soft Skills for the Workplace.

• Watch Soft Skills for Career Success.

• Explore Career Services resources at the University of Arizona Global Campus.

By Monday—Day 7

• Post two responses to peers in the Growth Mindset discussion forum.

• Post two responses to peers in the Soft Skills discussion forum.

• Complete the End-of-Course Survey.

• Complete the Self-Assessment Analysis assignment.

• Watch the Typefocus Introduction video that explains how TypeFocus Assessments can be useful in your career plans.

• Watch The Qualities of a Strong Leader to hear alumni leaders explain the skills they believe contribute to leadership success.

• Complete the TypeFocus Assessments.

• The full-text version of this eBook is available through the Constellation suite accessed through your online classroom, mobile app, or the Constellation website.
